title: [HIGH] provenance: no commit-signing policy or CI signature verification — supply-chain attestation missing before crates.io release
description:
Commit provenance + signing audit.

**Observation.** grep for `gpgsign|signed|ssh` in AGENTS.md returns nothing. grep for `commit.gpgsign` in the repo returns nothing. The Skill system prompt warned me explicitly: "NEVER skip hooks (--no-gpg-sign) unless user requests" — implying signing MAY be configured — but nothing in the repo requires it.

Verified via `git log --show-signature -5`: no SIGNED entries. Recent commits include both me (cc-pandas via `git -c user.name="cc-pandas"`) and the upstream Dicklesworthstone identity. Neither is cryptographically attested.

**Context — why this matters for a public library:**
- crates.io accepts publishes from anyone with API credentials + a matching Cargo.toml author. It doesn't verify commit signatures.
- docs.rs builds whatever source the tag points at. No signature check.
- The multi-agent swarm has several authorial identities mingling in `git log`. Auditing "did a real human approve this commit?" requires either (a) trusting `Author:` fields (which anyone can spoof) or (b) requiring signed commits.
- When the PyO3 binding ships (br-diic) + the release workflow lands (br-4clx), frankenpandas becomes a supply-chain dependency for downstream projects. Users of the Python wheel have no way to verify the tag that built it came from a trusted identity.
- Even within the swarm: if one compromised agent identity starts committing malicious code, reverting requires distinguishing legit commits from attack commits.

**Severity.** HIGH — supply-chain provenance for a library that will publish to crates.io. Low overhead to add; high cost to retrofit after a provenance incident.

**Fix shape:**

1. Documented policy in AGENTS.md + CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s):
   - All human contributors configure SSH commit signing (simpler than GPG, GitHub supports it since 2022):
       git config commit.gpgsign true
       git config gpg.format ssh
       git config user.signingkey ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
   - Swarm-agent identities publish their signing keys in a committed `AUTHORS.md` with public-key fingerprints. Identity-to-key mapping becomes auditable.
   - AGENTS.md gets a section: "How to prove your agent commits are yours, not spoofed."

2. CI gate:
   - ci.yml adds `- name: Verify commit signatures` running `git log --format='%G?' -1 <commit> | grep -q '^[GU]'` (G = good signature, U = good with unknown key).
   - Or: enforce via GitHub branch protection rule (toggle in repo settings) — requires signed commits to merge into main.

3. Release provenance:
   - br-4clx (release workflow) — tag signing: `git tag -s vX.Y.Z` with the release manager's key. crates.io publish reads the signed tag via the release-plz tooling; SLSA-level attestation becomes possible.
   - Consider Sigstore/cosign for supply-chain attestation alongside GPG signing — gives us a verifiable chain from source → tag → published crate.

4. Swarm agent identities:
   - Each agent that commits (cc-pandas, cod, etc.) has its own ssh signing key, committed public key in AUTHORS.md.
   - Commits tagged `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>` get the Co-Authored-By line verified via the primary author's signature chain.

Blast radius: AGENTS.md addition (+~30 lines), AUTHORS.md (new, ~20 lines for current identities), ci.yml (+1 step ~10 lines), repo settings (branch protection toggle — requires repo admin). Zero source code changes.

Related:
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — policy docs live here.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — private disclosure channel; the corresponding public provenance is addressed by this bead.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — tag signing integrated with the publish pipeline.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) + br-hg60 (secret scan) — the same supply-chain hygiene family.

title: [testing-metamorphic] [HIGH] DataFrame::transpose silently drops rows when source index has duplicate labels
description:
## Finding (metamorphic property: df.T.T == df)

DataFrame::transpose (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:21003) silently drops
data when the source DataFrame has duplicate index labels.

The transpose builds new columns into a BTreeMap<String, Column> keyed
by row-label-as-string. Duplicate labels (e.g. index = [10, 10, 20])
collapse via BTreeMap::insert (last write wins). Then
DataFrame::normalize_column_order (line 17504) actively de-duplicates
the new_order Vec, removing any repeated entries. Result: a 3-row
1-col DataFrame transposes to a 1-row 2-col DataFrame — silent data
loss, no error returned.

## Reproducer

  let mut cols = BTreeMap::new();
  cols.insert("a".to_owned(),
              Column::new(DType::Int64, vec![1.into(), 2.into(), 3.into()])?);
  let df = DataFrame::new_with_column_order(
      Index::new(vec![10_i64.into(), 10_i64.into(), 20_i64.into()]),
      cols, vec!["a".to_owned()])?;
  let t = df.transpose()?;
  // Expected (pandas): 1-row 3-col with column names ["10", "10", "20"]
  // Actual (fp-frame): 1-row 2-col with column names ["10", "20"]

## Pandas semantics (target)

  >>> df = pd.DataFrame({'a': [1,2,3]}, index=[10,10,20])
  >>> df.T.shape
  (1, 3)  # all 3 columns preserved, with duplicate name '10'

## Why the bug exists

DataFrame's column store is BTreeMap<String, Column>, which CANNOT
hold duplicate column names by construction. Transpose's output
shape conflict (rows-with-duplicate-labels → columns-must-be-unique)
is silently resolved by BTreeMap::insert collapse, dropping data
without any error.

## Fix options considered

1. (preferred) Detect duplicate index labels at the top of transpose()
   and return Err with a clear message. Prevents silent data loss;
   user can dedupe their index first or use a different approach.

2. Disambiguate duplicate names by appending .1/.2 suffixes. Would
   preserve all data but invents column names not in pandas output —
   stronger divergence than option 1.

3. Migrate column store to a structure supporting duplicate keys.
   Major architectural change; out of scope for this bug.

Shipping option 1: explicit-not-implicit. Silent data loss is
strictly worse than an error a user can see and route around.

## Tests

- transpose with duplicate index labels returns Err
- transpose preserves all rows when index has no duplicates
- metamorphic property: df.transpose().transpose() == df (holds when
  no duplicates; verified separately for the no-duplicate case)
- error path: error message clearly identifies the cause

## How surfaced

/testing-metamorphic skill applied to the involution property
df.T.T == df. Property holds trivially when index has no duplicates;
breaks under duplicate-label input — that's the canary that revealed
the silent-collapse bug.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 0bb9ded — transpose now rejects duplicate index labels with a clear error rather than silently collapsing columns. 1560 passed / 0 failed.

title: fd90.42: tighten autoincrement detection for composite PKs (defect from fd90.37)
description: fd90.37's autoincrement detection has a documented false positive: composite PK where the first column is INTEGER gets autoincrement=true even though SQLite's rowid-alias rule requires the column to be the SOLE primary key. Fix: tighten the heuristic from per-row 'pk==1 && type==INTEGER' to a two-pass check: first count columns with pk>0; only mark a column as autoincrement when it has pk==1 AND it's the only one with pk>0 AND its declared_type is INTEGER. The existing test sql_table_schema_autoincrement_not_set_for_composite_pk_integer documented the false positive — flip its assertion to verify the correct behavior. Add a confirming test that single-PK INTEGER and composite-PK with INTEGER first behave correctly. No struct API change — pure heuristic refinement inside the rusqlite override.

title: [LOW] collab: no .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ — bug/feature/conformance/perf reports not structured at filing time
description:
GitHub issue templates audit.

**Observation.** `ls .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` → directory does not exist. `.github/` contains only the `workflows/` directory.

**Context.** br-frankenpandas-6d5s (CODEOWNERS / PR template / CONTRIBUTING.md) umbrella-filed the PR-side collaboration infra. Issue templates were mentioned in that bead but not filed as a separate slice. Filing separately because the template structure differs materially from PR templates:

- Bug reports need a reproducer steps + expected-vs-actual structure + pandas-parity-divergence checkbox ("is this a conformance gap filed under DISCREPANCIES.md?").
- Feature requests have a different shape: "pandas method name / pandas docs link / minimal motivating example".
- Conformance divergence reports are a THIRD category — not bug (may be intentional) and not feature (existing behavior, just different). These route to DISCREPANCIES.md, not to the bug tracker directly.
- Performance regressions are a FOURTH category — reproducer should include `perf_baselines.rs` run output, baseline numbers, and ideally a bisect starting commit.

**Severity.** LOW — issue triage quality improves but nothing currently breaks without templates. GitHub's default "blank issue" works. Calibrating honestly.

**Fix shape.** `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` directory with four YAML templates:

1. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.yml` — GitHub issue forms (YAML, not markdown) with:
   - title: "[BUG] "
   - dropdown: Affected crate (fp-frame / fp-io / fp-conformance / ...)
   - textarea: Reproducer
   - textarea: Expected behavior (include pandas reference if relevant)
   - textarea: Actual behavior
   - checkbox: Have you checked DISCREPANCIES.md?
   - checkbox: Have you checked the bead tracker (br ready)?

2. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature_request.yml`:
   - title: "[FEATURE] "
   - input: pandas method name being mirrored
   - input: link to pandas docs
   - textarea: minimal motivating example
   - dropdown: Affected crate

3. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/conformance_divergence.yml`:
   - title: "[CONFORMANCE] "
   - textarea: Input that diverges
   - textarea: pandas output
   - textarea: frankenpandas output
   - dropdown: expected classification (MUST / SHOULD / MAY per br-nato)
   - note: "This issue will be considered for DISCREPANCIES.md entry."

4. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/perf_regression.yml`:
   - title: "[PERF] "
   - textarea: Before/after timing numbers
   - input: git bisect-good commit SHA
   - textarea: Reproducer (ideally a perf_baselines.rs-style bench)
   - dropdown: Affected operation class (GroupBy / Merge / Rolling / IO / ...)

5. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml`:
   - `blank_issues_enabled: false` — forces users into one of the 4 templates.
   - `contact_links:` — pointer to the bead tracker + "if you're an agent, use br create".

Blast radius: 1 new directory + 5 small YAML files (~30 lines each = ~150 lines total). Zero source changes. Zero CI changes.

Prerequisites:
- None urgent. Can ship independently.
- br-6d5s (CODEOWNERS + PR template umbrella) — natural coordination point.

**Related:**
- br-6d5s (CODEOWNERS / PR template / CONTRIBUTING.md umbrella) — this bead fills in the issue-template slice left open.
- br-niwb (error-path conformance) — the conformance_divergence template captures exactly the bug class niwb fixes at the test layer.
- br-nato (RequirementLevel) — the conformance template's MUST/SHOULD/MAY dropdown uses nato's taxonomy.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — security issues go through the private disclosure channel per 8k1i, NOT through these public templates. config.yml's contact_links should mention that.
close_reason: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ already shipped on main (5 yaml templates + config.yml).

title: [HIGH] fp-conformance 8 cascading aggregate tests fail on opaque assert_is_green; need fixture-level surface
description:
Workspace test (rch exec -- cargo test --workspace --all-targets) shows 351 passed / 8 failed in fp-conformance. The 8 failures all panic with terse messages like 'assertion failed: report.is_green()' or 'CI PIPELINE: FAILED (0/1 gates passed)' — the underlying fixture-level mismatches (37 cases per packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases) are buried inside the report payload only when {report:?} is included.

Affected tests (all in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs):
- ci_gate_g6_conformance_evaluates (line 29042)
- ci_pipeline_conformance_only_runs_and_reports (line 29063)
- differential_suite_produces_structured_drift (line 27256)
- failure_forensics_report_clean_when_all_pass (line 28519)
- e2e_suite_runs_full_pipeline (line 28054)
- grouped_reports_are_partitioned_per_packet (line 23210)
- packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases (line 20547)
- differential_all_packets_green (line 27457)

Fix: replace the bare assert!(report.is_green()) at each site with a structured failure dump that emits per-case (packet_id, case_id, mismatch_class, mismatch) when report.failed > 0. Pattern already used at 20943 in packet_filter_runs_groupby_aggregate_matrix_packet ('expected report green: {report:?}'). Apply the same pattern to all 8 sites. Bonus: write the dump to a JSONL artifact under artifacts/ so the reaper can ingest it.

Impact: turns 8 'opaque cascade' failures into actionable per-case records that downstream agents can pick off without re-running each top-level test.
close_reason: Completed

title: Add Series.cumprod conformance test coverage
[HIGH] release: 12 crates at 0.1.0 with no publish workflow or semver policy — library is structurally un-releasable
description:
Release / publish workflow + versioning audit.

**Observation.**
- All 12 crates sit at version = "0.1.0" (verified via grep). No crate has been bumped since the workspace was initialized.
- No `publish = false` set anywhere. Cargo's default is `publish = true`, which means any developer running `cargo publish -p fp-types` today would push 0.1.0 to crates.io. A moment later, `cargo publish -p fp-columnar` fails because fp-columnar has `fp-types = { path = "../fp-types" }` and path-dependencies can't be published. The publish ordering is undocumented.
- .github/workflows/ has exactly ONE file: ci.yml. No release.yml, no publish.yml, no tag-driven workflow.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` section per crate — there's a workspace-root CHANGELOG but no per-crate versioning story.
- No semver policy documented. The workspace edition is "2024" (Rust 2024 edition, nightly-only), so MSRV is unpinned — but semver needs a user-facing stability story.

**Consequence.**
- First release is impossible today without manual ceremony: 12 Cargo.toml bumps, dependency-path rewrite to `version = "..."` pinned cross-crate refs, cargo publish each in topological order, tag the commit, update Cargo.lock, repeat across all downstream crates. Any misstep breaks the publish.
- frankenpandas-diic bead (PyO3 bindings) depends on the core crates being publishable — the Python wheel pipeline needs `frankenpandas = "X.Y.Z"` from crates.io.
- Multi-agent swarm adds commits faster than any manual release process can keep up. If release is manual, releases never happen; users stay on git dependencies.
- Security fixes (future CVE patches) can't ship quickly because the pipeline doesn't exist.

**Severity.** HIGH — the product is structurally un-releasable today despite being 3171-tests-green. README markets "Add to your Cargo.toml: frankenpandas = { path = 'crates/frankenpandas' }" which is a path dep, not a released version. Users can't consume the library the way the README implies.

**Fix shape:**

1. Workspace-level versioning:
   - Every crate's Cargo.toml [package] version inherits from workspace via `version.workspace = true`.
   - Single source of truth in root Cargo.toml [workspace.package] version = "0.1.0" → bumped once per release.
2. Semver policy in CONTRIBUTING.md (sibling bead): pre-1.0 = minor bumps may break, patch bumps won't; post-1.0 = strict semver.
3. Cross-crate dep version pinning: [workspace.dependencies] gets one fp-types = { version = "0.1.0", path = "crates/fp-types" } entry each, so published crates carry a versioned dep and path-lookup happens only during local dev.
4. Release workflow `.github/workflows/release.yml`:
   - Trigger: git tag matching `v*`.
   - Steps: validate semver bump matches commit messages, run full CI suite, `cargo publish -p <crate>` in topological order (fp-types → fp-columnar → fp-index → fp-runtime → fp-types → fp-expr → fp-groupby → fp-join → fp-io → fp-conformance → frankenpandas), attach CHANGELOG.md diff to GitHub Release.
5. `cargo-release` (external tool) or `release-plz` (GitHub Action) automates the topological publish. Install in CI; configure via release-plz.toml.
6. Per-crate CHANGELOG files auto-generated by release-plz from conventional-commit prefixes (feat/fix/chore/etc — the prefixes are already in use based on recent git log).

Blast radius:
- 12 Cargo.toml edits (version.workspace = true flip, +1 line each).
- workspace Cargo.toml: add [workspace.package] version, add [workspace.dependencies] entries for each fp-* crate.
- 1 new .github/workflows/release.yml (~100 lines).
- 1 new release-plz.toml (~30 lines).
- 1 new section in CONTRIBUTING.md (covered by sibling CODEOWNERS bead).

Prerequisites:
- br-frankenpandas-36qc (cargo-audit) + br-frankenpandas-0l5r (cross-platform CI) ideally green before 0.1.0 ships — you don't want to publish a 0.1.0 that's untested on Windows.
- br-frankenpandas-1d9y (nightly pin) — MSRV claim can't happen without a pinned toolchain.

Once all prerequisites + this bead land, first release becomes `git tag v0.1.0 && git push --tags`.

title: [MEDIUM] parity: Timedelta arithmetic + TZ-aware datetime ops
description:
Parity-gap audit: datetime + Timedelta arithmetic, TZ-aware operations.

**Scope.** pandas:
- Timestamp + Timedelta → Timestamp.
- Timestamp - Timestamp → Timedelta.
- Timedelta + Timedelta → Timedelta.
- Timedelta * scalar → Timedelta.
- tz-aware: `pd.Timestamp('2024-01-01', tz='US/Eastern') + pd.Timedelta(days=1)` stays in the same tz.
- DST transitions: adding 1 day across a spring-forward boundary changes wall-clock hour but preserves absolute duration.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-types has `Timedelta` namespace with NANOS_PER_* constants + parse(). No Timestamp type surfaced publicly; datetime values live as Utf8-encoded ISO 8601 strings per README Limitations.

Gaps (from README + existing DISCREPANCIES):
- No native Timestamp dtype → TZ-aware arithmetic not possible.
- Timedelta arithmetic: `Timedelta + Timedelta` not exposed as a pub fn; only internals.
- Scalar::Timedelta64 exists but no `Scalar::Timestamp` / `Scalar::DatetimeTz`.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — datetime handling is pandas's foundational surface; partial coverage limits financial/time-series workflows. chrono-tz is already a workspace dep per Cargo.toml inspection, so IANA tz data is available.

**Fix shape.** Phased:
- Phase 1: expose Timedelta + Timedelta arithmetic as pub fns in fp-types.
- Phase 2: add Timestamp struct (i64 nanos since Unix epoch, optional chrono_tz::Tz).
- Phase 3: live-oracle fixtures for dt arithmetic: Timestamp + Timedelta, Timestamp - Timestamp, tz-aware shift across DST, leap-second handling.

title: [testing-fuzzing] [HIGH] Series::map / map_with_default / map_with_na_action / replace are O(n × m) — scan mapping vec linearly per element
description:
## Finding (fuzzing-lens worst-case input analysis)

Sister gap to br-frankenpandas-d8d9d/15f51/7c7d0 (the unique/dedup
O(n²) fixes). Four more functions have a `mapping.iter().find(...
.semantic_eq(val))` linear scan per input value:

  Series::map (line 3879)
  Series::map_with_default (line 3920)
  Series::map_with_na_action (line 6993)
  Series::replace (line 3995)

Each is O(n × m): n input values × m mapping entries scanned per
value. For n=1M, m=10K: ~10¹⁰ comparisons.

The fuzzing-input shape that exposes this: build a Series of n
random Int64s and a mapping of m random (Int64, Int64) pairs;
the call should be sub-second but isn't.

## Fix

Build a `HashMap<ScalarKey, &Scalar>` from the mapping ONCE; do an
O(1) lookup per input value. ScalarKey-based equivalence matches
semantic_eq for non-Float64 scalars (Int64, Bool, Utf8, Timedelta,
Null) within a homogeneous column.

## Float64 tolerance preservation (subtle)

Scalar::semantic_eq applies a **relative tolerance of 1e-14** to
Float64-vs-Float64 pairs. ScalarKey::FloatBits is bit-exact and
would miss `Float64(1e-15) ≈ Float64(0.0)` pairs that semantic_eq
catches. To preserve correctness:

- If any mapping key is Float64, fall back to the existing linear
  scan (correctness > perf for the rare float-key case).
- Otherwise, build the HashMap and use O(1) lookup.

This guard preserves semantic_eq's tolerance behavior exactly while
giving O(n) for the common case (Int64/Utf8/Bool keys, which are
the >99% case for map/replace usage).

NaN/Null collapse: ScalarKey already maps Float64(NaN) and
Null(NaN) to the same key, matching semantic_eq's NaN-as-missing
treatment.

## Reproducer (perf, not correctness)

  let mapping: Vec<(Scalar, Scalar)> = (0..10_000)
      .map(|i| (Scalar::Int64(i), Scalar::Int64(i * 10))).collect();
  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      (0..1_000_000).map(|i| (i as i64).into()).collect(),
      (0..1_000_000).map(|i| Scalar::Int64(i % 10_000)).collect())?;
  s.map(&mapping);     // before: minutes. after: <1s

## Tests

- map / map_with_default / map_with_na_action / replace correctness
  on small inputs (existing tests must remain green)
- new test: map with Float64 mapping key falls back to linear scan
  (preserves tolerance behavior)
- new test: replace with 1000 mapping entries on 5000-element Int64
  Series performs correctly (perf scaling implied by test size)
- new test: NaN/Null in input map to mapping's null bucket if
  present (semantic_eq null-collapse preserved)

## Why surfaced

/testing-fuzzing applied to find worst-case input shapes for the
mapping-based functions. The "n large, m large" fuzzing fixture
trivially exposes the O(n × m) shape that grep-only inspection
would miss.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 704f121 + fmt 358aa3c — Series::map / replace / map_with_default / map_with_na_action now O(n+m) via try_build_mapping_index helper. Float64-keyed mappings preserve semantic_eq tolerance via linear-scan fallback. 1591 passed / 0 failed.

title: fd90.206: Add SqlReadOptions/SqlWriteOptions/SqlInspector/read_sql_chunks to prelude
description:
fd90.206: Add SQL helper types + read_sql_chunks family to the prelude.

The README's IO Format Support table at line 148 documents the SQL row with these types:
- read_sql / read_sql_table (already in prelude)

User code following the README that calls `df.read_sql_chunks(&conn, "SELECT ...", chunk_size)?` or builds a `SqlReadOptions { params: ..., parse_dates: ... }` cannot do so via `use frankenpandas::prelude::*;` alone — they need explicit `use frankenpandas::{SqlReadOptions, SqlWriteOptions, SqlInspector, read_sql_chunks};`.

Scope:

- Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard
- Per scope discipline: only add the named types (don't sweep in unrelated SQL helpers)

Time estimate: 15-25min.

All four are documented in the README's IO Format Support table at line 148 (SQL row mentions 'SqlReadOptions (params, parse_dates, ...)', 'SqlWriteOptions (if_exists, index, ...)', 'SqlInspector for SQLAlchemy.Inspector-shaped introspection') but were exposed at the top-level only. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard with type-checks for each (SqlInspector via fn-pointer over &SqlInspector<'_, rusqlite::Connection>; SqlWriteOptions has no Default impl, used fn-pointer). All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: [mock-code-finder] [HIGH] Series::is_monotonic_increasing returns false for sorted Utf8/Bool — uses to_f64 instead of semantic_cmp like its sibling
description:
## Finding (mock-code-finder lens, sister-function inconsistency)

Series::is_monotonic_increasing (line 3889) and is_monotonic_decreasing
(line 3916) are sibling functions but use INCONSISTENT comparison logic:

  is_monotonic_increasing:
    match (pair[0].to_f64(), pair[1].to_f64()) {
        (Ok(a), Ok(b)) if a <= b => {}
        _ => return false,
    }

  is_monotonic_decreasing:
    if !a.semantic_ge(b) { return false; }

semantic_cmp (in fp-types) handles ALL Scalar variants including Utf8,
Bool, Int64, Float64, Timedelta64. to_f64 fails on Utf8/Bool, so
is_monotonic_increasing returns false even for sorted strings:

  pandas: pd.Series(["a","b","c"]).is_monotonic_increasing == True
  fp-frame: returns False (wrong)
  
  pandas: pd.Series([True,True,True]).is_monotonic_increasing == True
  fp-frame: returns False

The is_monotonic_decreasing impl IS correct for these dtypes —
is_monotonic_increasing is a stub of the same shape its sibling
already implements correctly.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Utf8("a".into()), Scalar::Utf8("b".into()),
           Scalar::Utf8("c".into())])?;
  s.is_monotonic_increasing();
    // before: false (to_f64 fails on Utf8)
    // after: true (matches pandas)

## Fix

Use semantic_le (the LE counterpart of semantic_ge already used in
is_monotonic_decreasing) so the increasing path uses the same
all-dtype logic. Same NaN/missing semantics as the decreasing path
(missing values bail out with false).

## Tests

- is_monotonic_increasing on sorted Utf8 → true
- is_monotonic_increasing on sorted Bool [false, true] → true
- is_monotonic_increasing on Int64 (regression guard) → true
- is_monotonic_increasing on Float64 (regression guard) → true
- is_monotonic_increasing on unsorted Utf8 → false
- is_monotonic_increasing on Utf8 with missing → false
- existing tests remain green

## Why surfaced

/mock-code-finder lens applied to sister-function consistency
checks. Two functions that should share comparison semantics — one
correct, one a stub. The to_f64-based path is the kind of stub that
silently fails on non-numeric input.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 559a9f2 — is_monotonic_increasing now uses semantic_le (matches sibling _decreasing's all-dtype logic). Sorted Utf8/Bool series now correctly identified. 1615 passed / 0 failed.

title: [testing-fuzzing] [HIGH] Series::idxmax/idxmin returns Err on all-NEG_INFINITY/all-INFINITY input; pandas returns first index
description:
## Finding (boundary-value analysis under /testing-fuzzing lens)

Series::idxmax (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:5062) initializes
best_val = f64::NEG_INFINITY and updates best_idx only when v > best_val.
For a Series where every non-missing value equals f64::NEG_INFINITY, no
strict-greater comparison ever succeeds, so best_idx stays None and the
function returns Err("idxmax of empty or all-null series") — even though
the series has valid values.

Same bug exists in:
- Series::idxmin (line 5039) with f64::INFINITY initialization
- SeriesGroupBy::idxmin (line 11203)
- SeriesGroupBy::idxmax (line 11226)
- DataFrame::idxmin/idxmax (lines 26055, 26075) inherit via delegation,
  silently emitting Null instead of the first label.

## Pandas semantics (target)

>>> import pandas as pd
>>> pd.Series([float('-inf'), float('-inf')]).idxmax()
0  # returns first index, NOT an error
>>> pd.Series([float('inf'), float('inf')]).idxmin()
0
>>> pd.Series([float('-inf')]).idxmax()
0

When all values are equal (including ±inf), pandas returns the FIRST
index — first-occurrence tie-breaking. fp-frame errors out instead.

## Why the bug exists

The condition `if v > best_val` skips equality, which is correct for
ties on regular floats (first-occurrence wins because subsequent equal
values fail the strict-greater check). But when best_val is initialized
to the sentinel ±INFINITY, the FIRST iteration's strict-greater check
also fails, leaving best_idx = None forever.

## Fix

Initialize best_idx on the first non-missing value regardless of strict
comparison:

  if best_idx.is_none() || v > best_val {
      best_val = v;
      best_idx = Some(i);
  }

This preserves the existing first-occurrence tie-breaking on regular
floats (subsequent v > best_val still fails on ties) while ensuring
best_idx is always set when at least one non-missing value exists.

## Repro
let s = Series::from_values("x", vec![0.into(), 1.into(), 2.into()],
    vec![Scalar::Float64(f64::NEG_INFINITY); 3])?;
s.idxmax();  // before: Err. after: Ok(0)

## Tests
- idxmax on all-NEG_INFINITY series → first index
- idxmin on all-INFINITY series → first index
- idxmax on mixed (regression guard for first-occurrence tie-breaking)
- groupby idxmax/idxmin on all-±inf groups

## How surfaced

/testing-fuzzing skill, boundary-value lens. Series::idxmax was scanned
because aggregator initial-accumulator patterns are a classic source of
edge-case bugs (init = sentinel + strict-comparison loop).
close_reason: Shipped fix in 18f345a — idxmax/idxmin now initialize best_idx on first non-missing value, handling all-±INFINITY input correctly. 1557 passed / 0 failed.

title: fp-types Timestamp floor_to/ceil_to/round_to (9p0u Phase 2.5)
description:
Phase 2.5 follow-up to br-9p0u (Timestamp scaffolding shipped in commit 574b4b5).

**Scope.** Add Timedelta-unit rounding methods on Timestamp:
- `Timestamp::floor_to(unit_nanos: i64) -> Timestamp` — round down to nearest multiple.
- `Timestamp::ceil_to(unit_nanos: i64) -> Timestamp` — round up.
- `Timestamp::round_to(unit_nanos: i64) -> Timestamp` — banker's rounding (half-to-even, matches pandas .round()).

**Reference behavior (pandas 2.2):**
- `pd.Timestamp('2024-01-01 12:34:56').floor('H')` → `2024-01-01 12:00:00`.
- `.ceil('H')` → `2024-01-01 13:00:00`.
- `.round('H')` → `2024-01-01 13:00:00` (banker's rounds half toward even).
- NaT → NaT for all three.
- Unit must divide pandas's representable range; nonsense units pandas rejects with ValueError.

**FrankenPandas state.** Phase 2 added `Timestamp` struct + add/sub Timedelta. No rounding helpers. Pure i64 arithmetic on nanos axis — no TZ math required (rounding-then-conversion vs convert-then-rounding can diverge for DST-crossing units, but the unit_nanos parameter constrains us to strictly numeric rounding which matches pandas's tz-naive semantics).

**Fix shape (Phase 2.5 — pure additive in fp-types):**
1. Three `pub fn` on Timestamp: `floor_to`, `ceil_to`, `round_to`.
2. Floor: `nanos.div_euclid(unit) * unit` (handles negative correctly).
3. Ceil: floor result + (unit if remainder != 0).
4. Round: distance-to-nearest-multiple; ties go to even multiple.
5. Zero/negative unit_nanos → returns NaT (matches pandas ValueError surface as NaT propagation).
6. Tests: 8-10 cases including positive/negative nanos, exact-multiple no-op, zero-unit rejection, NaT propagation.

**Blast radius.** Purely additive in fp-types. Zero call-site touches.

**Severity.** LOW — small sweetener for users porting pandas datetime rounding workflows. Phase 3 chrono_tz integration adds the tz-aware variant.

title: [HIGH] docs: CHANGELOG.md 671 commits / 12 days behind HEAD; stats table understates state by ~2-9x per row
description:
CHANGELOG.md maintenance audit.

**Observation.** `CHANGELOG.md` is 536 lines / ~48 KB. Last modified by `git log -1 -- CHANGELOG.md`: **2026-04-10 23:50:20** — "Harden IO column validation".

**Count of commits since last CHANGELOG update:** `git log --oneline <last-changelog-sha>..HEAD | wc -l` → **671 commits**.

**Stats table at the end of CHANGELOG.md (lines 527-536) is also wildly stale:**
  Total commits | 269                       ← reality: 269 + 671 = ~940
  Date range    | 2026-02-13 to 2026-03-17  ← reality extends to 2026-04-22 (today)
  Tags / releases | None (pre-release)       ← still true, but closer to 0.1.0
  Workspace crates | 12                     ← still correct
  Lines of Rust | 84,000+                    ← reality likely 120k+ given recent epics
  Public functions | 788                    ← reality: 1530 (verified 2026-04-22 via grep in br-7cfm)
  Conformance packets | 55+                  ← reality: 430 (verified 2026-04-22 in br-n609)

**Consequence.**
- CHANGELOG.md is the canonical summary a user hitting docs.rs / crates.io / GitHub Releases reads to understand what's in the project. It's 12 days + 671 commits behind reality. Every new user lands on an outdated picture.
- br-4clx (release workflow) will tag v0.1.0 with CHANGELOG.md as release-notes input. If we tag today, the notes say "55+ conformance packets" while reality is 430 — ~9x understatement.
- The CHANGELOG format (features / fixes / chore grouped by conventional-commit prefix) could be auto-generated by release-plz or git-cliff, but is currently hand-maintained. Nobody will keep up with 671-commits-every-12-days cadence by hand; the swarm's velocity outpaces manual documentation.
- br-4clx proposes release-plz for the publish workflow. release-plz IS the CHANGELOG automation — same tool. Landing 4clx closes this gap as a side-effect, but 4clx is itself blocked on br-1d9y (nightly pin), br-36qc (cargo-audit), br-0l5r (cross-platform CI). Meanwhile CHANGELOG keeps drifting.

**Severity.** HIGH — user-facing credibility at release. Also: a CHANGELOG that's stale for 12 days + 671 commits on an active swarm is effectively abandoned documentation, regardless of whether a release ships.

**Fix shape:**

1. Short-term (ship today or tomorrow): one catch-up commit. Use `git log --pretty='format:- %s'` between the last CHANGELOG sha and HEAD, filter by conventional-commit prefix (feat / fix / test / chore / docs), append to CHANGELOG under an "Unreleased" section. Regenerate the stats table using:
   - `git log --oneline | wc -l` (total commits)
   - `grep -c "^pub fn" crates/*/src/lib.rs` (public fns — use the script from br-7cfm once it ships, but a one-shot grep works for now)
   - `ls crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/*.json | wc -l` for packet count
   - `find crates -name '*.rs' | xargs wc -l` for LOC

2. Medium-term: adopt `git-cliff` (or release-plz) with a `cliff.toml` that maps conventional-commit prefixes to CHANGELOG sections. Run in CI as a "chore(changelog)" auto-commit on merge to main. Preserves the Unreleased section until `git-cliff --bump --tag v0.1.0` rolls it forward.

3. Link CHANGELOG entries to bead IDs: the project already uses `(br-frankenpandas-XXXX)` suffix on most commit subjects; `git-cliff` can extract those and link to `.beads/issues.jsonl` for traceability.

4. Once CI auto-commits the CHANGELOG, drop the hand-maintained stats table or auto-regenerate it (same script from fix 1).

Blast radius: 1 one-shot catch-up commit (~200-400 lines appended to CHANGELOG.md), +1 cliff.toml (~30 lines), +1 ci.yml job (~15 lines), +1 line in CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s) pointing at the new auto-regeneration flow. Zero source-logic changes.

Prerequisites:
- None urgent for the one-shot catch-up.
- br-4clx (release workflow) for the automated flow — they should ship together.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release workflow + release-plz) — this bead becomes moot after 4clx lands (release-plz handles changelogs natively). File separately so it can be closed quickly with a one-shot catch-up.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — the changelog policy lives here.
- br-n609 (COVERAGE.md auto-generator) + br-zk1j (API matrix) — sibling auto-generated-doc beads. All three replace hand-maintained stats.

title: fd90.152: FEATURE_PARITY.md packet evidence shows ~21 of 430 packets — add note pointing to actual ledger
description:
fd90.74-151 audit. FEATURE_PARITY.md (referenced from README's Key Documents at line 1666 as 'Detailed parity matrix with status per feature family') has a 'Phase-2C Packet Evidence' table at lines 51-75 listing only ~21 packets:
  - 11 FP-P2C packets (001-011)
  - 10 FP-P2D packets (025, 026, 027, 028, 029, 030, 031, 032, 039, 040)

But the actual fp-conformance fixture coverage is much larger:
  ls crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ | grep -oE 'fp_p2[cd]_[0-9]+' | sort -u | wc -l
  → 430 distinct packet suites
  - 11 FP-P2C (matches README exactly)
  - 419 FP-P2D (FEATURE_PARITY.md shows 10 of 419 = 2.4% coverage)

The table grew dramatically since FEATURE_PARITY.md was last refreshed; it's now misleading users who read it expecting comprehensive parity status.

Two ways to fix:
A. Auto-regenerate the table from artifacts/phase2c/ — out of scope for a 60-min slice (would need a script).
B. Add a clarifying note: 'Below is the foundational subset; the full live packet ledger is in artifacts/phase2c/drift_history.jsonl with 430 total packet suites.'

Going with B for this slice — the README's Conformance section already gives the actual count. Pure docs-only fix to align expectations.

title: [HIGH] collab: no CODEOWNERS / PR template / CONTRIBUTING.md despite active multi-agent swarm — zero review-routing infrastructure
description:
Contributor / collaboration infrastructure audit.

**Observation.** `.github/` contains exactly one directory (workflows/) and nothing else. Confirmed absences:
  .github/CODEOWNERS                      — no review routing
  .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md        — no PR checklist
  .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/                 — no issue templates
  /CONTRIBUTING.md                        — no contributor guide
  /CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md                     — no code of conduct
  /SECURITY.md                            — no disclosure (br-8k1i covers separately)

**Context.** This repository is running an ACTIVE multi-agent development swarm — that's not speculation. Evidence from today's git log alone:
- 20+ commits across 6 hours from multiple agents (cc-pandas + Dicklesworthstone + parallel workers).
- br-frankenpandas-iy8u: the original bead was a doctrine note about agents bundling co-landed work with misleading titles. Closed today by c9e9fff ("docs(workflow): require pre-commit diff stat and co-landed bead IDs").
- br-frankenpandas-lxhr (monolith bead): cites rebase conflicts between parallel agents on fp-frame/src/lib.rs as a primary pain point.
- Pre-commit hooks at `.git/hooks/hooks.d/pre-commit/50-agent-mail.py` prove the swarm coordinates through agent-mail infra.

Despite the swarm being active, there is NO documented process for:
- Which agent reviews which area of code (no CODEOWNERS).
- What a PR must include before merge (no PR template).
- How external contributors (humans) onboard (no CONTRIBUTING.md).
- What behavior is expected in issues / PRs (no CODE_OF_CONDUCT).
- How scientific / pandas-behavior-divergence discussions should be classified (no issue template distinguishing bug / feature / conformance-question / docs).

**Consequences already visible:**
- br-iy8u root cause (cod-style commits bundling co-landed work) persists as oral doctrine rather than enforceable CODEOWNERS routing.
- New slice-1 child bead (1zzp.1) had two agents working the same file simultaneously; the CODEOWNERS + PR template would have routed one to review the other's draft rather than both editing in parallel.
- Contributor onboarding is zero-documented: a new agent (this cc-pandas session is literally "fresh cc on new account" per the opening user message) had to reconstruct workflow from context clues.

**Severity.** HIGH — infrastructure that compounds across every PR. Every day without it, the swarm eats overhead re-discovering which file to edit and which agent to block on.

**Fix shape:**

1. `.github/CODEOWNERS`:
   - `/crates/fp-types/ @core-types-owner` (or an agent alias like `@cc-frankenpandas-types`)
   - `/crates/fp-frame/ @frame-domain`
   - `/crates/fp-io/ @io-domain`
   - `/crates/fp-conformance/ @conformance-domain`
   - `/.github/workflows/ @ci-owners`
   - `/.beads/ @scrum-bot` (if there's a bead-curator agent)
   - Auto-routes PR review requests; works with the existing multi-agent workflow.

2. `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md`:
   - Required: linked bead ID (br-frankenpandas-*)
   - Required: conformance / fuzz / performance impact checkbox
   - Required: `git diff --stat` summary (directly addresses br-iy8u doctrine) — autopopulate via the pre-commit hook already in place
   - Required: which live_oracle_* tests the PR touches / exercises
   - Required: acceptance criteria from the child bead

3. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md`, `feature_request.md`, `conformance_divergence.md`:
   - Conformance divergences get their own template because they flow to DISCREPANCIES.md, not to a regular bug tracker.

4. `CONTRIBUTING.md`:
   - How to install rch + cargo-fuzz + pandas oracle.
   - How to file a bead (`br create --no-db ...` per today's doctrine).
   - AGENTS.md link for swarm-specific workflow.
   - How to run conformance (`rch exec -- cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib`).
   - How to regenerate fixtures (once br-boyr ships the pipeline).
   - Semver and release expectations (once br-4clx ships).

5. `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`: boilerplate Contributor Covenant. Five minutes to add.

Blast radius: 5 new files, no source code changes. Each file is independently useful — can ship as 5 small PRs instead of one.

Related:
- br-frankenpandas-iy8u (workflow doctrine) — already closed via the c9e9fff commit that added pre-commit diff-stat requirement. This bead captures the codepath-level enforcement (CODEOWNERS), the human-facing documentation (CONTRIBUTING), and the PR-time enforcement (template) that the doctrine bead acknowledged were needed.
- br-frankenpandas-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — sibling file in the /github root; coordinate rollout.

title: fd90.13: SqlConnection schema-introspection probes
description: fd90 sub-bead: add SqlConnection schema-introspection probes for backends that support multi-schema isolation. Phase 1 adds supports_schemas() -> bool (default false) and default_schema() -> Option<String> (default None) with rusqlite override returning false/None (SQLite has a single schema namespace per file connection). PostgreSQL/MySQL backends override with true + 'public' / 'information_schema' or per-connection search_path. Unblocks pd.read_sql_table(.., schema='X') routing in fd90 phase 2. Pure additive, default impls. 3-4 tests.

title: fd90.9: SqlConnection capability + dialect probe methods
description:
fd90 sub-bead: extend SqlConnection trait with backend-capability + dialect probes (default impls).

**Scope.** The existing fp-io SqlConnection trait (lib.rs:~3074) defines query / execute_batch / table_exists / insert_rows / dtype_sql / index_dtype_sql. To host PostgreSQL + MySQL backends behind the same trait without leaking concrete types, we need backend-introspection methods that the read_sql / read_sql_table machinery can dispatch on.

**Phase 1 (this slice — purely additive, default impls):**
- `dialect_name(&self) -> &'static str` — returns "sqlite" / "postgresql" / "mysql" / etc. Default: "unknown".
- `supports_returning(&self) -> bool` — true when backend honors INSERT ... RETURNING. Default: false (conservative).
- `max_param_count(&self) -> Option<usize>` — backend-specific upper bound on bound-param count per statement (SQLite: 32766, MySQL: 65535, PostgreSQL: 65535). Default: None (unbounded).
- `with_transaction<T, F: FnOnce(&Self) -> Result<T, IoError>>(&self, f: F) -> Result<T, IoError>` — txn context. Default impl: just calls f (no BEGIN/COMMIT — caveat in rustdoc that backends should override).

**Override on impl SqlConnection for rusqlite::Connection:**
- dialect_name → "sqlite".
- supports_returning → true (SQLite 3.35+ supports RETURNING).
- max_param_count → Some(32766).
- with_transaction → wraps in `self.execute("BEGIN")` / `self.execute("COMMIT")` with rollback on Err (closure must return Result).

**Tests.** 4-5 unit tests covering the rusqlite override + default-impl behavior on a struct that doesn't override.

**Blast radius.** Pure additive in fp-io/src/lib.rs. Zero call-site touches (consumers haven't started using these methods yet — Phase 2 wires them into the read_sql dispatch path).

**Severity.** P1 — keystone for PG/MySQL backend impls (fd90 epic deliverable 1 from the bead body).

title: [reality-check] add pandas.DataFrame.tz_convert + tz_localize
description:
**Reality-check finding** (artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md).

`tz_convert` and `tz_localize` exist on `DatetimeAccessor` (Series-level) but not on DataFrame. Pandas users call `df.tz_convert('UTC')` to convert all datetime columns at once.

Implementation: walk all datetime columns and apply DatetimeAccessor methods column-by-column.

title: [code-review] [HIGH] read_sql_table_columns_with_index_col requires manual index_col inclusion — pandas auto-projects
description:
**Severity:** HIGH (correctness regression vs pandas parity, in production code path).

**Finding:** Two public helpers in `crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs` require the caller to manually include `index_col` in the `columns` list, while pandas `read_sql_table(table, con, columns=[...], index_col=...)` auto-projects the index_col into the underlying SELECT.

Affected:
- `read_sql_table_columns_with_index_col` (line 6294) — explicit docstring: "When `index_col` is set it must be included in `columns`"
- `read_sql_table_columns_chunks_with_index_col` (line 6329) — same docstring requirement

If a caller does what pandas allows (`columns=["name"], index_col="id"` where `id` is NOT in `columns`), the call fails with `"index_col 'id' not present in result columns"` raised by `promote_column_to_index` (line 5499).

**Why this is a regression now:** commit 48a2f0b (`fix(fp-io): include SQL index_col in table projections (frankenpandas-fd90.76)`) just landed the auto-projection fix for the options-based path:
- `read_sql_table_with_options` ✓ now auto-projects via new `sql_table_read_query_for_options` helper
- `read_sql_table_with_options_and_index_col` ✓ same
- `read_sql_table_chunks_with_options_and_index_col` ✓ same

But the standalone `*_columns_with_index_col` helpers still go through `read_sql_table_columns(conn, table_name, columns)` → no auto-projection. The two API styles now have inconsistent behavior.

**Reproducer (sketch, would need fp-io tests):**
```rust
// fails: "index_col 'ints' not present in result columns"
read_sql_table_columns_with_index_col(&conn, "tbl", &["names"], Some("ints"))?;
// works (existing test pattern):
read_sql_table_columns_with_index_col(&conn, "tbl", &["names", "ints"], Some("ints"))?;
// pandas equivalent (works):
// pd.read_sql_table("tbl", con, columns=["names"], index_col="ints")
```

**Suggested fix:** route both `*_columns_with_index_col` helpers through `sql_table_read_query_for_options`-style logic so they auto-project the index_col. Either:
1. Inline a sibling function for the columns-list signature, OR
2. Have `read_sql_table_columns_with_index_col` build a temporary `SqlReadOptions` and delegate to `read_sql_table_with_options_and_index_col`.

Update both docstrings to drop the "must be included in columns" requirement, and add a regression test mirroring fd90.76's `read_sql_table_with_options_columns_auto_project_index_col`.

**Discoverer:** cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) during review-mode audit on 2026-05-02, P1 STUDY of commit 48a2f0b.
close_reason: Code already shipped in ad56d0d: columns-list SQL table readers auto-project index_col and regression tests cover full/chunked paths.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_rolling_window_with_min_periods target (window/min_periods edge cases)
description:
Series::rolling and DataFrame::rolling expose (window, Option<min_periods>) but no fuzz target exercises the boundary conditions where window > len, min_periods=0, min_periods > window, all-NaN windows, or window=usize::MAX (panic-trigger candidate for arithmetic overflow).

Proposed target: fuzz_rolling_window.rs:
1. Synth mode: 1 numeric Series, 4-32 rows, NaN injection rate from input bytes.
2. window picked as min(N+1, raw_byte) so we exercise window > len; min_periods picked from a separate byte (None / 0 / 1 / window / window+1).
3. Apply each rolling agg in {sum, mean, min, max, std, var, count, first, last, prod, median}.
4. Asserts: output length == input length; all values prior to (window - 1) where min_periods is at default are NaN; never panics for any valid (window, min_periods) combination.

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_rolling_window_bytes(input: &[u8]). Stresses the partial-window handling that recent grouped-rolling work (br-x5oy/9uuq/4hkt) only tested at 2-3 fixed window sizes.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: verified parallel agent's commit e909f29 implements the bead spec. fp_conformance::fuzz_rolling_window_bytes (lib.rs:6550) projects byte inputs into a bounded numeric-or-missing Series via fuzz_rolling_series_from_bytes, then runs Series::rolling(window, Some(min_periods)).{sum,mean,min,max,std,var,count,first,last,prod,median}() across 11 aggfuncs — exceeds the bead's 6-aggfunc spec. window_tag modulo 6 hits the edge-case grid {0, 1, len, len+1, usize::MAX, N} via fuzz_rolling_window_from_byte; min_periods_tag modulo 6 hits {None, 0, 1, window, window+1, N} via fuzz_rolling_min_periods_from_byte — covering every edge case the bead names (window=0, min_periods>window, window>series_len, min_periods=0, window=series_len). fuzz_validate_rolling_result locks three invariants: output length == input length, window=0 must reject via FrameError::CompatibilityRejected without panic, and min_periods>window => all-missing output. fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_rolling_window.rs wraps the harness under libfuzzer_sys. Also added fp-frame Rolling::first / last / prod to complete the aggfunc grid (commit e909f29 crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs +46 lines). Tests: 3/3 fuzz_rolling_window_bytes_* seed-assertion unit tests pass under rch (accepts_empty_input, accepts_window_zero_seed, accepts_min_periods_gt_window_seed).

title: fd90.107: README df.transform("zscore") wouldn't compile — DataFrame::transform takes closure, not string
description:
fd90.74-106 README audit. Line 649 in 'Multiple ways to apply custom logic' shows:

  let normed = df.transform("zscore")?;

But crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:19590 declares:
  pub fn transform<F>(&self, func: F) -> Result<Self, FrameError>
  where F: Fn(&Scalar) -> Scalar

DataFrame::transform takes a closure, NOT a string. Passing "zscore" wouldn't compile. The string-based transform(func_name: &str) ONLY exists on DataFrameGroupBy (per the doc-comment 'Matches df.groupby(col).transform(func_name) semantics'). All surrounding examples in the same code block (applymap, apply_row, assign_fn, pipe) correctly use closures.

Fix: rewrite the example to use a closure returning a Scalar of the same dtype, matching the actual signature. Or replace with the GroupBy variant if showing a string-based transform makes more sense in that paragraph. Pure README change.

title: fd90.220: Add SqlInsertMethod to prelude
description:
fd90.220: Add SqlInsertMethod to the prelude.

SqlInsertMethod (Single/Multi) is the type of `pub method: SqlInsertMethod` field on SqlWriteOptions. README documents `pd.to_sql(.., method='multi')` semantics for the multi-row VALUES batching path. SqlWriteOptions is in the prelude (fd90.206), but SqlInsertMethod was not — same defect family as fd90.219 (CsvOnBadLines).

User code:
    let opts = SqlWriteOptions { method: SqlInsertMethod::Multi, ... };

cannot resolve SqlInsertMethod from prelude alone, even though the consuming SqlWriteOptions is reachable.

Scope:
- Add SqlInsertMethod to the prelude (top-level already has it via fp_io re-export at line 76)
- Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 5-10min.
close_reason: Added SqlInsertMethod (Single/Multi) to the prelude. Same pattern as fd90.219 (CsvOnBadLines): SqlWriteOptions has 'pub method: SqlInsertMethod' but the enum was reachable only at top-level (not in prelude). User code building SqlWriteOptions { method: SqlInsertMethod::Multi, ... } from the prelude could not resolve the enum. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: fd90.40: SqlInspector::reflect_table for bundled table metadata
description: Add SqlReflectedTable struct (table_name, columns, primary_key_columns, indexes, foreign_keys, unique_constraints, comment) and SqlInspector::reflect_table(table, schema) -> Result<Option<SqlReflectedTable>, IoError> that bundles all 5 introspection calls into one fetch. Mirrors SQLAlchemy.MetaData.reflect_table(). Returns Ok(None) when the table doesn't exist (i.e. table_schema returns None). Pure-delegation slice — calls table_schema, primary_key_columns, list_indexes, list_foreign_keys, list_unique_constraints, table_comment in sequence. Tests cover unknown table -> None, real SQLite table populating columns + PK + index, multi-schema PG-like routing, comment propagated through bundle.

title: fd90.149: README claims 30+ FP-P2D packets through 055; actual is 419+ packets through 433
description:
fd90.74-148 README audit. Conformance Testing in Depth section at line 1449 claims:

  '... plus 30+ DataFrame-level packets (FP-P2D-014 through FP-P2D-055) covering merge, concat axis options, head/tail, loc/iloc, sort, constructor variants, and more.'

But the actual fp-conformance fixture packet count for P2D is much higher:
  ls crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ | grep -oE 'fp_p2d_[0-9]+' | sort -u | wc -l
  → 419 distinct P2D packet IDs

And the range:
  earliest: fp_p2d_014
  latest:   fp_p2d_433 (verified with sort -u | tail)

So:
- '30+ DataFrame-level packets' → actually 419+
- 'FP-P2D-014 through FP-P2D-055' → actually 014 through 433

The README's range claim caps at 055 — off by a factor of ~10× on count and ~7-8× on range.

Fix: update both the count ('30+' → '400+') and the range ('014 through 055' → '014 through 433+') to reflect actual coverage. The list of operations covered ('merge, concat axis options, head/tail, loc/iloc, sort, constructor variants, and more') is fine.

Pure README change. No code impact.

title: [testing-fuzzing] [HIGH] Series::resample silently falls back to daily bucketing for unknown freq strings; pandas raises ValueError
description:
## Finding (input-shape fuzzing lens)

Resample::bucket_key (line 10116) has a wildcard fallback:

  match freq {
      "Y" | "A" => Some(year.to_string()),
      "M" => Some(format!("{year}-{month}")),
      "D" => Some(date_part.to_string()),
      _ => Some(date_part.to_string()),  // ← silent fallback
  }

Any unknown freq ("W", "INVALID", "weekly", etc.) silently behaves
like "D" — daily bucketing. Pandas raises:

  pd.Series([1.0]).resample("INVALID").mean()
  → ValueError: Invalid frequency: INVALID

Sister gap to br-d895b (ewm input validation) and br-7c962 (rolling
min_periods validation) — same family of silent-fallback-as-stub.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![IndexLabel::Utf8("2024-01-01".into())],
      vec![Scalar::Float64(1.0)])?;
  s.resample("INVALID").mean();
    // before: silently returns daily-bucket result
    // after:  Err("resample: invalid frequency 'INVALID'; supported: Y, A, M, D")

## Fix

Add a Resample::validate() method that errors on unknown freq.
Public methods (mean, sum, count, min, max, etc.) call validate()
first via the existing aggregate_scalar helper.

  fn validate(&self) -> Result<(), FrameError> {
      match self.freq.as_str() {
          "Y" | "A" | "M" | "D" => Ok(()),
          other => Err(FrameError::CompatibilityRejected(format!(
              "resample: invalid frequency '{other}'; supported: Y, A, M, D"
          ))),
      }
  }

aggregate_scalar invokes validate() before bucket-building. The
match arm inside bucket_key keeps the same shape but the wildcard
becomes unreachable in practice. (Could be tightened further but
out of scope; goal is matching pandas's ValueError contract.)

## Tests

- resample("INVALID").mean() → Err mentioning "invalid frequency"
- resample("W").mean() → same (W not currently supported in fp-frame)
- resample("Y").mean() / "A" / "M" / "D" all succeed (regression
  guard for the supported subset)
- existing resample tests with valid freq remain green

## Why surfaced

/testing-fuzzing input-shape lens — when a method takes a `&str`
and only handles a small enum-like subset, fuzzing the input string
trivially exposes silent fallbacks. This is the resample equivalent
of br-d895b's ewm and br-7c962's rolling validation gaps.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 85541b5 — Resample::validate now rejects unknown freq strings per pandas. Validation called from all Result-returning methods. 1672 passed / 0 failed.

title: [HIGH] docs: 0 of 1530 pub fns document '# Panics:' + no cargo doc gate in CI — public API contract underspecified
description:
Rustdoc + panic-contract audit across the public API surface.

**Numbers (fresh grep across crates/*/src/lib.rs):**
- Total `pub fn` definitions: 1530
- pub fns documenting `# Panics:`: 0
- cargo-doc step in ci.yml: absent
- `#![warn(missing_docs)]` or `#![deny(missing_docs)]` in any crate root: absent
- RUSTDOCFLAGS in ci.yml: unset (so `-D warnings` for rustdoc isn't applied)

**Observation.** fp-frame alone has 3836 `unwrap()` / `expect()` / `panic!` / `unreachable!` sites (fp-io: 546; fp-expr: 131). The overwhelming majority are in `#[test]` bodies (grep-verified on a sample) which is fine. BUT with zero `# Panics:` rustdoc comments across 1530 pub fns, a user cannot mechanically determine:
- Which pub fns assume non-empty Series and panic on empty?
- Which pub fns panic on mixed-dtype input vs returning Err?
- Which pub fns assume sorted indices?

The library's own conformance harness uses `.expect("value")` patterns (see fp-io/src/lib.rs:3663+), which is correct for test code but sets no pattern for user-facing docs.

**Consequence.** When a downstream Python binding (planned per br-diic) or a Rust library user hits an undocumented panic, they have no spec to reference. The `# Panics:` contract is pandas-aligned too: pandas' equivalent is "raises ValueError" in its rustdoc-analog (docstrings), and users rely on it.

**Severity.** HIGH — public API contract is underspecified. Downstream integrations (PyO3 wrapper when it ships, other Rust libs) have to read source to learn what panics vs errors.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add `#![warn(rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links)]`, `#![warn(rustdoc::missing_doc_code_examples)]`, and `#![warn(missing_docs)]` to each `crates/*/src/lib.rs`. Start as warn, promote to deny once the doc backlog clears.
2. Add ci.yml step: `RUSTDOCFLAGS="-D warnings" cargo doc --workspace --no-deps --all-features`. Fails on any rustdoc warning.
3. Audit pass: for each pub fn that can panic (not returning `Result`), add a `# Panics:` section. Prioritize fp-frame public API — the largest public surface.
4. Optional: use `#[cfg_attr(debug_assertions, track_caller)]` on panicking pub fns so panic locations are clear in user tracebacks.

Blast radius: crate root attribute flip in 12 crates, ci.yml (+1 doc step), audit pass (~500-800 lines of doc additions; parallelizable across agents, one pub fn at a time).

First-wave priority order: fp-frame > fp-io > fp-expr > fp-groupby > fp-join (most-used downstream).
close_reason: Added workspace rustdoc CI gate and first-wave public # Panics docs in fp-frame

title: [testing-fuzzing] [HIGH] Series::idxmin/idxmax error on Utf8 input; sister gap to min/max Utf8 fix
description:
## Finding (fuzzing-input-shape lens)

Sister gap to br-frankenpandas-83c2a (Series::min/max Utf8 fix):

Series::idxmin (line 5545) and idxmax (line 5573) use val.to_f64()
which fails on Utf8 input — same stub shape that min/max had
before 83c2a. The Utf8 input shape is exactly what reveals the bug.

Pandas:
  pd.Series(['b','a','c']).idxmin() → 1 (index of lex-min 'a')
  pd.Series(['b','a','c']).idxmax() → 2 (index of lex-max 'c')

fp-frame: returns Err.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![10_i64.into(), 20_i64.into(), 30_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Utf8("b".into()), Scalar::Utf8("a".into()),
           Scalar::Utf8("c".into())])?;
  s.idxmin();  // before: Err. after: Ok(IndexLabel::Int64(20))
  s.idxmax();  // before: Err. after: Ok(IndexLabel::Int64(30))

## Fix

Branch on column dtype at the top of idxmin/idxmax. For Utf8, scan
strings lexicographically tracking the index of the running extremum.
Same shape as the min/max Utf8 fix. The to_f64 fallback path is kept
for numeric dtypes (already correct from br-5c98a).

The br-5c98a "first-non-missing-wins" semantics must be preserved on
the Utf8 path too (handles single-element Utf8 series; analogous to
all-NEG_INFINITY in numerics).

## Tests

- idxmin on Utf8 returns label of lex minimum
- idxmax on Utf8 returns label of lex maximum
- idxmin/idxmax on Utf8 with missing values skip the null
- idxmin/idxmax on all-null Utf8 series returns Err (matches existing
  all-null sentinel for numeric dtypes)
- Metamorphic property: s.values()[s.idxmin() position] == s.min()
  on Utf8

## Why surfaced

/testing-fuzzing — Utf8 input is a fuzzing-shape that hadn't been
exercised against idxmin/idxmax. Discovery driven by the same stub
pattern fixed in 83c2a (min/max), c4898 (cummin/cummax), 526a2
(is_monotonic_increasing): every numeric-via-to_f64 fallback hides
a Utf8 bug behind it.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 1a8567b — Series::idxmin/idxmax now support Utf8 lexicographic per pandas. Sister to 83c2a/c4898/526a2. 1633 passed / 0 failed.

Same hidden-type pattern as fd90.218: CsvReadOptions has 'pub on_bad_lines: CsvOnBadLines' field but the enum to set it was unreachable from frankenpandas. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: [HIGH] observability: zero tracing / log / #[instrument] integration — users can't introspect 'why is my groupby slow'
description:
Observability / tracing-integration audit.

**Observation.** grep across the entire workspace:
  - `tracing::`                 → zero matches
  - `::info!`, `::warn!`, `::debug!`, `::trace!` → zero matches
  - tracing / log / env_logger / slog in Cargo.toml → zero matches

Zero observability infrastructure. Every fp-* public function runs silently. The ONLY instrumentation is the `EvidenceLedger` type (used inside RuntimePolicy-bound operations) which records alignment decisions at the dataframe-logic level — not a general-purpose tracing / metrics surface.

**Context on why this matters for a pandas-parity library:**
- pandas users have a tradition of interactive tuning — "why is my groupby slow?" is answered by `%%time`, `pd.set_option('display.max_rows', ...)`, etc. The Rust equivalent for integrated profiling is `tracing::span!` + a `tracing-subscriber` layer. We ship neither the spans nor hooks for the subscriber.
- `Profile-proven performance` claim in README: for users to VERIFY the profile, they need introspection into where time goes. Without tracing, they must wrap every call in `Instant::now()` manually.
- br-diic (PyO3 bindings) will expose fp-frame to Python users. Python users DO have mature observability (logging, sys.settrace, cProfile). The Rust backend must emit spans the Python wrapper can bridge to those — see [PyO3 tracing example](https://github.com/PyO3/pyo3/discussions/2270). Without in-Rust spans, the Python profiler sees a single "fp_frame.groupby_sum" call taking 200ms and can't drill down.
- br-bahw (concurrency fuzz) + future rayon integration: concurrent sections need `tracing::instrument` to attach spans to tasks. Without spans, concurrent debugging is impossible.
- br-d6xa / br-qi6y (live oracle CI) — conformance gates produce JSON artifacts. Tracing spans would emit structured events parallel to the JSON artifacts, letting ops correlate "which test ran what" at scale.

**Skill rules / industry norms:**
- Tokio, Axum, sqlx, arrow itself — all integrate `tracing` spans at public entry points.
- Rust API Guidelines don't mandate tracing, but for library-sized crates (more than ~5k LOC) it's expected.
- `#[instrument]` macro is zero-cost when the subscriber discards the span (compile-out via features + proc-macro).

**Severity.** HIGH — library maturity + release-readiness gap. Adding tracing later is expensive (every public fn becomes a touch point); adding it now is mechanical.

**Fix shape:**

1. Add `tracing = { version = "0.1", default-features = false, optional = true }` to [workspace.dependencies].

2. Each fp-* crate adds a `tracing` optional feature:
   ```toml
   [features]
   default = []
   tracing = ["dep:tracing"]
   ```

3. Every pub fn that does non-trivial work gets `#[tracing::instrument(skip(...), fields(rows = frame.index().len(), ...))]`. Priority order:
   - fp-frame::DataFrame::groupby / .sum / .agg_named / .agg_multi
   - fp-frame::Series::rolling / .resample / .expanding
   - fp-io::read_* / write_* (both CSV / JSON / Parquet / Excel / Feather / Arrow IPC / SQL)
   - fp-join::join_series / join_dataframes
   - fp-expr::eval_str / query_str / parse_expr
   - fp-conformance: harness entry points (run_packet_suite, run_e2e_suite) — ALREADY have structured logging via the EvidenceLedger; convert + bridge.

4. Feature-gate the `tracing` dep in downstream crates so users without tracing don't pay the dependency cost.

5. Document in README + CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s): "Enable tracing via `cargo add frankenpandas --features tracing` and attach a `tracing-subscriber`. See examples/trace_my_groupby.rs."

6. Example: `examples/trace_my_groupby.rs` shows the 3-line subscriber setup + sample output.

7. Bridge for PyO3 (once br-diic lands): `pyo3-tracing` translates Rust spans into Python logging records.

Blast radius:
- Cargo.toml updates across 12 crates (+~60 lines total).
- `#[tracing::instrument]` annotations on ~50 high-traffic pub fns (+50-100 lines).
- 1 new example file (~60 lines).
- CONTRIBUTING.md addition (~20 lines).
- Zero source-logic changes. All `tracing::event!(...)` calls compile out when the feature is off.

Prerequisites: None. Can ship incrementally — one crate at a time, one pub fn at a time.

**Related:**
- br-diic (PyO3 bindings) — tracing spans propagate to Python via pyo3-tracing; adding spans now = Python debuggability later.
- br-7cfm (rustdoc + panic contract) — tracing spans at pub fn entry naturally pair with `# Panics:` docs as "when this pub fn is called, here's what you observe + what can go wrong."
- br-bahw (concurrency fuzz) + future rayon — concurrent sections need spans to be debuggable.
- br-ing6 + br-xha7 (perf gates) — tracing spans feed criterion's per-span reporting; same infrastructure as the bench gate.
close_reason: Phase 1 scaffolding: tracing optional workspace dep + fp-frame feature gate + CONTRIBUTING.md consumer docs. #[instrument] annotations on hot paths deferred to follow-up slices per crate.

title: fd90.244: Add read_sql_query + read_sql_table_chunks family to prelude
description:
fd90.244: Add read_sql_query / read_sql_table_chunks variants to the prelude.

The prelude has read_sql/read_sql_chunks/read_sql_table/read_sql_with_options. Several other user-facing SQL readers exposed at top-level are missing from prelude:

- read_sql_query (standalone query form — no table-name lookup)
- read_sql_query_with_options
- read_sql_query_chunks
- read_sql_query_chunks_with_options
- read_sql_table_chunks (table-based chunked iteration)
- read_sql_table_chunks_with_options
- read_sql_table_with_options

Scope (keep tight):
- Add the 4 most user-relevant variants to the prelude:
  read_sql_query, read_sql_query_with_options, read_sql_table_chunks, read_sql_table_with_options
- Skip the *_with_index_col + *_with_options_and_index_col variants — they're more specialized

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 10-15min.
close_reason: Added 4 SQL reader variants to the prelude: read_sql_query (standalone form, no table-name lookup), read_sql_query_with_options, read_sql_table_chunks (table-based chunked iteration), read_sql_table_with_options. Round out the SQL reader surface beyond the existing read_sql/read_sql_chunks/read_sql_table/read_sql_with_options. Skipped the *_with_index_col + *_with_options_and_index_col specialized variants. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: fd90.154: clarify parity_gate vs lib-test pass semantics in auto-generated table
description:
fd90.74-153 follow-on. The auto-generated table in FEATURE_PARITY.md (added by fd90.153) shows '1 passing / 430 total' as the headline — which is technically what parity_gate_result.json reports, but misleadingly low. Lib-level cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib reports 557 passing / 5 failing. The two metrics measure different things:

  - parity_gate_result.json 'pass': per-packet gate threshold (typically 0 fixture failures required)
  - cargo test --lib pass: individual #[test] functions returning Ok

A user glancing at the table will see '1 passing / 430 total' and conclude the project is mostly broken — but the lib test pass rate is ~99%.

Fix: add a clarifying preamble to the auto-generated block explaining the parity-gate semantics + linking to the lib-level pass rate. The headline aggregate counts stay (they're accurate for what they measure), but framed honestly so readers don't misinterpret.

Pure docs/script change in scripts/gen_feature_parity_table.py + regenerate FEATURE_PARITY.md. No code/behavior change to fp-frame/fp-conformance.

title: [testing-metamorphic] [HIGH] Series::min/max error on Utf8 input; sister gap to cummin/cummax Utf8 support
description:
## Finding (metamorphic property cummax()[-1] == max())

After br-frankenpandas-c4898 added Utf8 support to cummin/cummax,
the metamorphic property `cummax(s)[-1] == max(s)` (last running
max equals overall max) is testable — but max() still errors on
Utf8 because Series::min and Series::max have Int64 and Bool
specialized branches but fall through to a Float64-only path:

  let v = val.to_f64().map_err(ColumnError::from)?;

For Utf8 input, to_f64() fails → returns Err.

Pandas:
  pd.Series(['b','a','c']).min() → 'a'
  pd.Series(['b','a','c']).max() → 'c'

fp-frame: returns Err.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Utf8("b".into()), Scalar::Utf8("a".into()),
           Scalar::Utf8("c".into())])?;
  s.min();  // before: Err. after: Ok(Utf8("a"))
  s.max();  // before: Err. after: Ok(Utf8("c"))

## Fix

Add a Utf8 branch (after the Bool branch) in both Series::min and
Series::max that scans the values lexicographically. Same shape as
the Int64 and Bool branches; same shape as the cummin/cummax fix
from br-c4898.

Use Option<&str> as the running extremum (like the cummin/cummax
fix), with first-non-missing-wins initialization and clone-out at
the end into Scalar::Utf8.

## Tests

- min on Utf8 returns lexicographic minimum
- max on Utf8 returns lexicographic maximum
- min/max on Utf8 with one missing skips the null
- min/max on all-null Utf8 returns Float64(NaN) (matching the
  existing all-null sentinel return)
- existing Int64/Bool/Float64 tests must remain green
- metamorphic property cummax(s)[-1] == max(s) holds on Utf8

## Why surfaced

/testing-metamorphic — the property cummax(last) == max held trivially
when both sides errored on Utf8. With cummax fixed in c4898, max
became visibly broken: the metamorphic check exposed the divergence.
close_reason: Shipped fix in ef3f616 — Series::min/max now support Utf8 lexicographic ordering, matching pandas. Closes the metamorphic gap with cummin/cummax. 1628 passed / 0 failed.

title: [testing-fuzzing] [HIGH] Series::str.get_dummies has two O(n²) loops; switch to HashSet for token dedup and per-row membership
description:
## Finding (fuzzing input-shape, hot-loop scan-and-find)

Series::str.get_dummies (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14479) has two
quadratic loops in series:

1. all_tokens dedup (line 14495):
     for t in &tokens {
         if !all_tokens.contains(t) {
             all_tokens.push(t.clone());
         }
     }
   For N rows × avg_tokens per row × k unique-tokens:
   O(N × avg_tokens × k).

2. Output column membership (line 14516):
     for token in &all_tokens {
         let vals: Vec<Scalar> = row_tokens
             .iter()
             .map(|tokens| Scalar::Int64(i64::from(tokens.contains(token))))
             .collect();
     }
   For k columns × N rows × avg_tokens: O(k × N × avg_tokens).

Both are catastrophic. For N=1M, avg_tokens=5, k_unique=10K:
each pass is ~5×10¹⁰ string comparisons.

## Fix

(1) HashSet membership for all_tokens dedup, parallel Vec for
    insertion order (same shape as the recent 0d6f8/db6ec/0528d
    fixes).

(2) Store per-row tokens as HashSet<String> alongside the Vec.
    Output-column build then does O(1) membership lookup per cell
    instead of O(avg_tokens) Vec::contains.

## Reproducer (perf, not correctness)

  let s = ... 10K rows × ~5 tokens each, 5K unique tokens ...;
  s.str().get_dummies(",");
    // before: minutes (10⁹+ comparisons)
    // after:  ~milliseconds

## Tests

- get_dummies on small input gives expected indicator columns
  (regression guard for the existing-correct path)
- get_dummies on wide input (5K rows × 100 unique tokens)
  correctness asserted (perf scaling implied by test size)
- get_dummies preserves first-seen token order

## Why surfaced

/testing-fuzzing input-shape lens — long-string lists with many
unique tokens trivially expose the O(n²). Same scan-and-find
family as br-7c7d0/15f51/d8d9d/0d6f8/db6ec/0528d.
close_reason: Shipped fix in fb3dd65 — Series::str.get_dummies's two O(n²) loops replaced with HashSet membership; ~5×10¹⁰ → ~N × (avg_tokens + k). 1714 passed / 0 failed.

title: fd90.217: Expose MergeAsofOptions + merge_asof_with_options + JoinExecutionOptions
description:
fd90.217: Expose MergeAsofOptions + merge_asof_with_options + JoinExecutionOptions to top-level + prelude.

Three fp-join exports are completely hidden from frankenpandas:

- MergeAsofOptions (pub fields: allow_exact_matches, tolerance, by) — README claims "match pandas merge_asof parameters". The simple merge_asof in the prelude defaults all of these.
- merge_asof_with_options (consumer of MergeAsofOptions)
- JoinExecutionOptions (use_arena, arena_budget_bytes — performance tuning for join_series)

User code following the README's pandas-compatibility claim that wants to tune asof tolerance:
    let options = MergeAsofOptions { tolerance: Some(60.0), ..Default::default() };
    merge_asof_with_options(&left, &right, "ts", AsofDirection::Backward, options)?

cannot resolve either type or function from frankenpandas (top-level OR prelude).

Scope:
- Add the 3 items to the top-level fp_join re-export
- Add the same 3 to the prelude
- Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 15-25min.
close_reason: Exposed MergeAsofOptions, merge_asof_with_options, and JoinExecutionOptions at top-level + prelude. All three existed in fp-join but were entirely hidden from the frankenpandas crate. MergeAsofOptions has pub fields (allow_exact_matches, tolerance, by) matching pandas merge_asof parameters. merge_asof was in the prelude as a 4-arg simple form, but tuning via the options struct was unreachable. JoinExecutionOptions controls arena allocation for join_series. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard with type-checks. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: [porting-to-rust] [HIGH] Series::isin doesn't cross-match Bool with Int64/Float64 numeric test values; pandas does
description:
## Finding

Last tick (br-frankenpandas-f7201) fixed isin's O(n × m) but explicitly
documented and PRESERVED a correctness divergence: fp-frame's IsinIndex
treats Bool and numeric (Int64/Float64) variants as separate sets, so
Bool(true) doesn't match Int64(1) test value (and vice versa).

Pandas semantics (target):
  pd.Series([True, False]).isin([1, 0])
    → [True, True]  (Bool(true) matches Int64(1); Bool(false) matches Int64(0))
  pd.Series([True, False]).isin([1.0, 0.0])
    → [True, True]  (Bool ↔ Float64 also cross-matches)
  pd.Series([1, 0]).isin([True, False])
    → [True, True]  (reverse direction)

fp-frame returns [False, False] in all three cases — wrong.

## Why this exists

The existing scalar_isin_matches' fallback `_ => value == test_value`
relies on derived PartialEq, which distinguishes variants. Only Int64↔
Float64 was hand-wired for cross-matching. Bool was missed.

## Fix (porting pandas semantics)

In IsinIndex::contains, augment the per-variant branches:

- Bool(b) input: also check `ints.contains(&i64::from(b))` and
  `float_bits.contains(&(i64::from(b) as f64).to_bits())`.

- Int64(i) input where i is 0 or 1: also check
  `bools.contains(&(i != 0))`.

- Float64(f) input where f is exactly 0.0 or 1.0: also check
  `bools.contains(&(f != 0.0))`.

This matches pandas's "treat Bool as 0/1 for numeric comparisons"
semantics, while keeping all the previous-tick perf wins (single
hash-set build + O(1) lookup).

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Bool(true), Scalar::Bool(false)])?;
  s.isin(&[Scalar::Int64(1), Scalar::Int64(0)])?;
    // before: [false, false]
    // after:  [true, true] (matches pandas)

## Tests

- Series::isin Bool series, Int64 test values: cross-matches
- Series::isin Bool series, Float64 test values: cross-matches
- Series::isin Int64 series with 0/1 values, Bool test values: matches
- Series::isin Float64 series with 0.0/1.0 values, Bool test values: matches
- Int64(2) NOT matching Bool test (only 0/1 cross-match)
- Update the just-shipped series_isin_bool_does_not_cross_match_int test
  to assert the correct pandas-faithful output

## Why surfaced

/porting-to-rust applied to the bead-documented divergence from tick
f7201. The fix is a straightforward port of pandas's bool-as-0/1
semantics to the IsinIndex hash structure.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 4963055 — IsinIndex::contains now cross-matches Bool with Int64(0|1) and Float64(0.0|1.0) per pandas. Replaces the divergence preserved by f7201. 1600 passed / 0 failed.
