title: [MEDIUM] crates.io: 11 of 12 Cargo.toml carry no description/keywords/categories/repository — release-day discoverability gap
description:
crates.io metadata audit.

**Observation.** Out of 12 Cargo.toml files in the workspace, ZERO carry `keywords`, `categories`, `repository`, `homepage`, `documentation`, or `readme`. Only the umbrella `frankenpandas` crate has a `description` field; the 11 fp-* crates have none.

**Impact at crates.io release (br-4clx prerequisite).**
- `description`: crates.io shows "(no description given)" on search results + crate pages. Users scrolling browse results skip.
- `keywords`: crates.io keyword search (e.g. "dataframe", "pandas", "analytics") misses the crate. Up to 5 keywords per crate; each is a free discoverability channel.
- `categories`: crates.io has a curated category hierarchy (database::implementations, data-structures, science, etc.). Without it, the crate doesn't appear in any category browse.
- `repository`: no "Source" button on the crate page. Users can't jump from crates.io to GitHub.
- `homepage`: separate from repository — for projects with a dedicated website (frankensuite-website-development skill implies one exists).
- `documentation`: docs.rs auto-links via crate name so this is non-blocking, but setting it explicitly lets us point to a custom hosted docs if we ever diverge from docs.rs.
- `readme`: per-crate README pointer. By default it auto-detects but it's the kind of thing that silently breaks after a refactor.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — release-day discoverability gap. Not a correctness issue; not a data-safety issue; not a performance issue. Honest calibration: fixing after release means users who adopted 0.1.0 see outdated metadata until 0.1.1 ships; fixing before release is free.

**Fix shape.**

1. Workspace-level `[workspace.package]` gets a shared baseline:
   ```toml
   [workspace.package]
   edition = "2024"
   version = "0.1.0"
   license-file = "LICENSE"
   repository = "https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas"
   homepage = "https://frankenpandas.dev"          # if the site exists
   documentation = "https://docs.rs/frankenpandas"
   readme = "README.md"
   keywords = ["dataframe", "pandas", "analytics", "rust", "columnar"]
   categories = ["database-implementations", "data-structures", "science"]
   ```

2. Each fp-* Cargo.toml [package] inherits via `.workspace = true`:
   ```toml
   [package]
   name = "fp-frame"
   version.workspace = true
   edition.workspace = true
   repository.workspace = true
   homepage.workspace = true
   documentation.workspace = true
   readme.workspace = true
   keywords.workspace = true
   categories.workspace = true
   license-file.workspace = true
   description = "DataFrame and Series core for the frankenpandas workspace."
   ```

   Per-crate description remains bespoke (each fp-* crate does a different thing; workspace-level description would be generic). Keywords can be workspace-inherited because the discoverability terms overlap across crates.

3. Per-crate descriptions to draft (each ≤ 200 chars per crates.io limit):
   - fp-types: "Core Scalar, DType, NullKind primitives for the frankenpandas workspace — the universal type system behind fp-frame."
   - fp-columnar: "Columnar storage primitives (Column, ValidityMask) for pandas-parity dataframes."
   - fp-index: "Flat and multi-level row index types (Index, MultiIndex) for pandas-parity dataframes."
   - fp-runtime: "Runtime policies, evidence ledgers, and asupersync interop for the frankenpandas execution layer."
   - fp-frame: "DataFrame and Series with pandas-API parity and AACE index alignment."
   - fp-io: "IO layer: CSV, JSON, Parquet, Excel, Feather, Arrow IPC, SQL (SQLite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned)."
   - fp-groupby: "GroupBy engine for fp-frame with dense / arena / hash-map execution paths."
   - fp-join: "Merge and join engine (AG-05 leapfrog triejoin) for fp-frame DataFrames."
   - fp-expr: "Expression eval / query parser for fp-frame DataFrames (pandas df.eval/df.query parity)."
   - fp-conformance: "Conformance harness: differential test suite against live pandas oracle."
   - fp-frankentui: "FrankenTUI surface for interactive fp-frame workflows."

4. Categories per crate: mostly shared ("data-structures", "science") but IO crate can claim "filesystem" / "database-implementations"; runtime crate can claim "concurrency"; groupby/join can claim "algorithms".

Blast radius: workspace Cargo.toml + 11 per-crate Cargo.toml edits (~5-10 line additions each). Zero source changes. Per-crate descriptions need proofreading (~1 min each).

Prerequisites:
- br-4clx (release workflow) — this metadata is discovered at publish time; landing this bead BEFORE 4clx's first cargo publish saves a 0.1.1 patch.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release + workspace version inheritance) — same commit wave; natural pairing.
- br-60du (CHANGELOG stale) — another release-day metadata bead.
- br-wskz (docs.rs metadata) — companion: wskz controls docs.rs build config; this bead controls crates.io page content.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — repository URL in Cargo.toml points at where SECURITY.md lives.

title: fd90.260: Expose CSV/SQL index-cols reader variants to top-level
description:
fd90.260: Expose CSV/SQL index-cols reader variants to top-level.

Five fp-io readers are not exposed in frankenpandas:
- read_csv_with_index_cols (line 1141) — string + multi-column index promotion
- read_csv_with_index_cols_path (line 1164) — path variant
- read_sql_with_index_col — single index column
- read_sql_table_with_index_col — table-based with index_col
- read_sql_table_columns — list columns from a SQL table

These match pandas pd.read_csv(..., index_col=[0, 1]) and pd.read_sql_table(..., index_col=...) patterns.

Scope (keep tight):
- Add 5 readers to top-level fp_io re-export
- Don't add to prelude (more specialized than the basic variants already in prelude)

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 10-15min.
close_reason: Exposed 5 fp-io readers at the top-level frankenpandas re-export: read_csv_with_index_cols (string + multi-column index promotion), read_csv_with_index_cols_path (path variant), read_sql_with_index_col, read_sql_table_with_index_col, read_sql_table_columns (list columns from a SQL table). Match pandas pd.read_csv(..., index_col=[0,1]) and pd.read_sql_table(..., index_col=...) patterns. Kept out of the prelude (more specialized than the basic variants already there). All 36 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: fd90.140: README FrameError row lists dead DuplicateIndexUnsupported (never constructed); should show real wrappers
description:
fd90.74-139 README audit. Error Architecture table at line 826 lists FrameError::DuplicateIndexUnsupported as a 'Key Variant':
  | FrameError | fp-frame | LengthMismatch { index_len, column_len }, DuplicateIndexUnsupported, CompatibilityRejected(String) |

But the variant is declared at crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:113 and NEVER CONSTRUCTED anywhere in the workspace. Verified with:
  grep -rnE 'DuplicateIndexUnsupported\b' crates/  →  only the declaration, no usages

The error message ('duplicate index labels are unsupported in strict mode for MVP slice') suggests it was reserved for future strict-mode work that hasn't materialized. Listing it as 'key variant' that users will pattern-match is misleading — they'd never see it returned today.

The enum is #[non_exhaustive] so it could be removed from code, but the post-MVP intent makes that risky to preempt. Cleaner to update the README to show variants users actually will encounter:

Real variants users see today:
- LengthMismatch { index_len, column_len }
- CompatibilityRejected(String)  -- the catch-all returned by most validation failures
- Column(ColumnError)             -- transparent wrapper from #[from] ColumnError
- Index(IndexError)               -- transparent wrapper from #[from] IndexError

Fix: drop DuplicateIndexUnsupported from the FrameError row; add the two transparent wrappers (Column, Index) which users WILL match against. Pure README change.

title: [reality-check] make plotting decision — pandas plot/hist/boxplot on DataFrame/Series/GroupBy (out-of-scope or implement?)
description:
**Reality-check finding** (artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md).

Plotting methods currently return zero coverage:
- DataFrame: `plot, hist, boxplot`
- Series: `plot, hist`
- DataFrameGroupBy: `boxplot, hist, plot`
- SeriesGroupBy: `plot, hist`

That's 11 method gaps. Pandas delegates to matplotlib; in Rust the natural target is plotters.rs or charming.

**Decision needed** (file follow-ups based on choice):
1. **Out of scope**: document plotting as non-goal, add explicit `Err(NotImplemented("plotting is intentionally out-of-scope; use plotters.rs / charming directly"))` stubs so callers get a typed error rather than method-not-found. Update README.
2. **In scope, defer**: add stubs returning `NotImplemented`, file an implementation epic against plotters.rs / charming.
3. **In scope, implement**: full integration with a chart library; renders to PNG/SVG bytes at minimum.

Prereq: stakeholder decision on Vision Goal coverage. Given the AGENTS.md mandate ("ABSOLUTE AND TOTAL feature and functionality overlap"), option 1 is technically at odds with the vision. Option 2 is the safe middle ground.

**Discoverer**: cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) reality-check 2026-05-03.
close_reason: Completed: chose in-scope deferred plotting surface; public hooks now return typed compatibility errors, README documents plotters/charming backend deferral, and fp-frame regression tests cover DataFrame/Series/GroupBy plotting hooks.

title: fd90.233: Add direct df.min/max/std/var/median/prod/skew/kurt/sem methods (parity)
description:
fd90.233: DataFrame is missing direct min/max/std/var/median/prod/skew/kurtosis/sem reduction methods (pandas parity gap).

Discovered while writing fd90.232: DataFrame has 7 direct reduction methods (sum, mean, count, nunique, abs, all, any) but is missing the rest of the pandas-equivalent surface:

Pandas has:
- df.min() / df.max() — per-column min/max as Series
- df.std() / df.var() — per-column std/var (ddof=1 default)
- df.median() — per-column median
- df.prod() — per-column product
- df.skew() / df.kurt() / df.kurtosis() — higher-order moments
- df.sem() — standard error of mean

In fp-frame, these are reachable per-column (iterate columns, call Series.min()/etc.) or via df.describe() which produces them as a matrix. Direct DataFrame methods would be the natural pandas-parity ergonomic.

Note: df.min() / df.max() at fp-frame:12142/12147 LOOK like DataFrame methods but are actually inside `impl DatetimeAccessor`. The Iterator::min() trait fallback also fails to compile cleanly on `df.min()`.

This is a pandas-parity feature gap, not a regression. Filing for tracking; not for immediate implementation since the workaround (via columns or describe) exists.

Scope (when implemented):
- Add 9 reduction methods to `impl DataFrame {` returning per-column Series, matching the existing sum/mean shape.
- Match pandas defaults (ddof=1 for std/var/sem; numeric_only auto-detection).
- Add integration test coverage.
close_reason: Added 10 pandas-parity bare-name reduction methods to impl DataFrame: min, max, std, var, median, prod, skew, kurt, kurtosis (alias of kurt), sem. Each is a one-line delegation to the existing *_agg method (min_agg/max_agg/etc. were named _agg-suffixed because of an earlier name-resolution issue, but inherent methods shadow the trait fallback). The existing _agg method docstrings already claimed pandas parity ('Matches pd.DataFrame.min()'), but the user-facing names didn't match — fd90.233 closes that gap. Extended readme_element_wise_operations_compiles_and_runs to exercise all 10. All 1434 fp-frame lib tests + 35 integration + 7 frankenpandas lib + 2 doctests pass.

title: [HIGH] security: no secret scanning in CI or pre-commit — leaked credentials in commits become permanent git history
description:
Secret / credential hygiene audit.

**Observation.**
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml`: no gitleaks, no truffleHog, no detect-secrets, no similar scanner.
- Repo has ONE pre-commit hook: `.git/hooks/hooks.d/pre-commit/50-agent-mail.py` (coordination for the multi-agent swarm, not a secret scan).
- No `.pre-commit-config.yaml` (the pre-commit.com framework) — so no ecosystem-standard secret hooks either.
- No `.gitallowed` / `.gitleaksignore` / `.git-crypt` / `.gitattributes` with secret-relevant entries — nothing that suggests prior secret-scan discipline.

**Context.** This is a public-library repo that will ship to crates.io (once br-4clx lands). The multi-agent swarm produces 20+ commits/day from multiple authors. Real surface areas where secrets leak:
- CI artifacts: a test or fixture that inadvertently embeds a token (env var leaked into a string literal).
- Examples in rustdoc (doctests) pulling from real services with hardcoded API keys for demo purposes.
- Oracle script `oracle/pandas_oracle.py`: if it ever calls a remote service (it currently doesn't, but nothing prevents a PR adding one with a key).
- Sidecar config in `artifacts/`: JSON reports with paths, usernames, or host identifiers that constitute PII/infra info.

**Severity.** HIGH — git history is append-only for practical purposes. A secret committed today is discoverable by any scraper forever. Cleanup requires force-pushes (destructive, see skill rules about confirmations) plus rotation of every leaked credential.

**Fix shape:**

1. CI job `security`: runs gitleaks on every PR and on the `main` branch tip. Fails CI on any detection.
   ```yaml
   - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
     env:
       GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   ```
   Alternatively `trufflehog` for broader pattern coverage. Either is one step (~15s runtime).

2. Pre-commit hook `.git/hooks/hooks.d/pre-commit/20-secret-scan.sh`:
   - Runs `gitleaks protect --staged` on every commit.
   - Exits non-zero on finding.
   - Extends the existing 50-agent-mail.py single-hook pattern. Use numeric prefix ordering so secret scan runs before agent-mail (fast-fail on credentials before coordinating the commit with other agents).

3. `.gitleaks.toml` config:
   - Allowlist obviously-non-secret patterns (fixture data, example API URLs, placeholder strings).
   - Tighten rulesets for AWS / GCP / GitHub / npm / Docker Hub / Slack webhook patterns.

4. Documentation in CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s): "Never commit real credentials. Use env vars for CI secrets via GitHub Actions secrets. All test fixtures must use placeholder tokens starting with `test_` or `fake_`."

5. One-time retroactive scan: run gitleaks against the entire repo history NOW (before the gate goes live) so any prior leaks can be rotated.

Blast radius: 1 new CI job, 1 new pre-commit hook, 1 new .gitleaks.toml, 1 section in CONTRIBUTING.md. Zero source code changes beyond any secrets that the retroactive scan surfaces.

Related:
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — disclosure policy for external reports; this bead prevents us leaking secrets of our own.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — where the documented policy lives.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) — detects vulns in deps; this bead detects secrets in our own commits.

title: fd90.222: Add ArithmeticOp + ComparisonOp to prelude
description:
fd90.222: Add ArithmeticOp + ComparisonOp to the prelude.

Both enums are public types from fp_columnar and exposed at the top-level (line 29). They're parameter types for user-facing methods that ARE in the prelude:
- DataFrame.compare_scalar(scalar, op: ComparisonOp) — fp-frame:2232
- DataFrame.compare_scalar_df(scalar, op: ComparisonOp) — fp-frame:24766
- Column.binary_numeric(right, op: ArithmeticOp)
- Column.binary_comparison(right, op: ComparisonOp)

User code calling df.compare_scalar(&Scalar::Int64(10), ComparisonOp::Gt) from the prelude cannot resolve ComparisonOp — same hidden-field-type pattern as fd90.219/220.

Scope:
- Add ArithmeticOp + ComparisonOp to the prelude
- Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 10-15min.
close_reason: Added ArithmeticOp (Add/Sub/Mul/Div/Mod/Pow/FloorDiv) and ComparisonOp (Gt/Lt/Eq/Ne/Ge/Le) to the prelude. Both are parameter types for user-facing methods that are already in the prelude: Column.binary_numeric/binary_comparison, DataFrame.compare_scalar/compare_scalar_df, Series.compare_scalar. Same hidden-parameter-type pattern as fd90.219/220. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: [BUG] HyperLogLog::estimate() integer overflow on high rho values
description: HLL registers can store rho values up to 64 (from leading_zeros()), but estimate() used 2_u32.pow(r) which overflows when r > 31 (panic in debug, wrap in release → incorrect cardinality estimates). Fixed by using 2_f64.powi(). Added test for high rho values. All 66 fp-groupby tests pass. Commit: 2497512

title: [REALITY-CHECK HIGH] proptest prop_excel_round_trip_preserves_shape FAILS — public default round-trip broken
description: rch exec cargo test --workspace exposed prop_excel_round_trip_preserves_shape at crates/fp-conformance/tests/proptest_properties.rs:9746 FAILS with Excel round-trip must preserve column count: actual=2 expected=1. Same root cause as br-989b/br-mxho — fp_io::write_excel_bytes default index=true emits row index as leading column; fp_io::read_excel_bytes default does not promote it back. Default-pair round-trip leaks an extra column. Conformance harness was patched but public defaults still broken AND proptest catches it. Validates LOW arnn green-for-the-wrong-reason and ESCALATES to HIGH. Fix: make read_excel_bytes default detect-and-strip leading index column OR change write default to index=false (diverges from pandas; needs sign-off). Add fp-io level default-pair round-trip test.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: fixed in-tree via commit d0c5b2f. fp-io added infer_writer_emitted_default_excel_index_col which detects the writer-emitted default leading RangeIndex column (header auto-generated + values 0,1,2,... matching row position) and strips it back to the DataFrame index during read_excel_bytes. prop_excel_default_round_trip_preserves_shape flipped to assert shape preservation (no more +1 extra column). Also retires the green-for-wrong-reason state arnn flagged: assert_excel_roundtrip now uses write_excel_bytes default + read_excel_bytes default and passes. Tests: 2/2 fp-io excel_default_* unit tests pass (excel_default_read_promotes_writer_range_index_back_to_index + excel_default_read_keeps_non_range_generated_leading_column_as_data); 3/3 excel proptests pass (prop_excel_default_round_trip_preserves_shape + prop_excel_round_trip_preserves_shape_when_index_false + prop_excel_round_trip_int64_exact).

title: fd90.16: schema-qualified DROP TABLE in write_sql Replace branch
description: fd90 sub-bead: schema-qualified DROP TABLE in write_sql's SqlIfExists::Replace branch. Currently the Replace path uses 'DROP TABLE IF EXISTS quote_sql_ident(table)' which (a) bypasses the conn.quote_identifier dispatch (so MySQL backticks etc. don't take effect) and (b) doesn't schema-qualify even when SqlWriteOptions.schema is Some on a multi-schema backend. fd90.15 already routes CREATE/INSERT through schema; this slice plugs the same gap on DROP. Add private helper sql_drop_table_query_in_schema(conn, table, schema) and use it from the Replace branch. 3 tests: bare DROP on stub, qualified DROP on multi-schema stub, SQLite silent-ignore round-trip via write_sql Replace + schema=Some.

Multi batches rows into a single multi-row INSERT statement, chunked by conn.max_param_count() when set. Tests cover Single default, Multi chunking at param-count boundary, NaT/Null preservation, and fallback when max_param_count returns None.

title: [code-review] commit 0cdbe80 reopened 36 beads with newer closed entries — JSONL race overrides legitimate closures
description:
**Finding:** Commit `0cdbe80` ("chore(beads): reopen stale in-progress claims", 2026-05-02T21:36 EDT) flipped 36 JSONL entries from `in_progress` → `open` based on the rule "no commits in 6h + no live agent reservation". For 6 of the 36 (all `frankenpandas-cod1m01/cod1m02/cod1m03/cod1d13/cod2-79f08a56/cc1e01`), the JSONL **already contained a newer `closed` entry** with a populated `close_reason` and a corresponding fix commit on main. The reopen flip overrode the legitimate closure because JSONL semantics use latest-updated-at as winner.

**Concrete cases (verified by `git show 0cdbe80~1:.beads/issues.jsonl` and `git log --grep=<id>`):**

| ID | Pre-`0cdbe80` newest | Fix commit on main |
|---|---|---|
| `frankenpandas-cod1d13` | closed @ 2026-04-28 | `bc169e2` docs(fp-conformance): move DISC-013 to Resolved Divergences |
| `frankenpandas-cod1m01` | closed @ 2026-04-28 | `fe06882` fix(columnar): match pandas modulo signs |
| `frankenpandas-cod1m02` | closed @ 2026-04-28 | `99100b3` fix(runtime): replace placeholder raptorq envelope |
| `frankenpandas-cod1m03` | closed @ 2026-04-28 | `1ac00fc` test(frame): freeze display goldens |
| `frankenpandas-cod2-79f08a56` | closed @ 2026-04-28 | `6e68080` fix(conformance): clean SQL fuzz clippy |
| `frankenpandas-cc1e01` | closed @ 2026-04-29 | `9f32270` test(conformance): add series autocorr live oracle |

**Plus 19 frankenscipy-* entries in the same diff.** Those belong in a different repo's beads anyway (per close_reason on `frankenscipy-ul3v`: "imported in error from scipy - belongs in frankenscipy db, not frankenpandas").

**Impact:**
- bv triage now reports 25 false-positive open beads → top_picks=0, blockers_to_clear=0, actionable=0 → triage looks dead.
- Subsequent agents pick these up as "ready work" and either re-do done work or get stuck investigating (this happened to me: cc-pandas / CopperHarbor on 2026-05-02T21:50 EDT).

**Suggested remediation:**
1. Triage script must check for newer closed entries before reopening: only flip to open if (status=in_progress AND no more recent closed entry exists in JSONL AND no fix commit references the bead-id).
2. Re-close the 6 frankenpandas-* beads in the JSONL with `close_reason` pointing to the fix commit hash.
3. Move the 19 frankenscipy-* entries out of the frankenpandas JSONL (or mark closed with the "imported in error" reason).

**Discoverer:** cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) / CopperHarbor on 2026-05-02 during review-mode triage.
close_reason: Shipped fix in commit a5f6916; DB lookup blocked by stale blocked_issues_cache (br doctor)

title: [MEDIUM] concurrency: Index uses std::cell::OnceCell (!Sync); blocks Arc<DataFrame> sharing + future rayon work
description:
Deadlock / concurrency audit finding (post-/deadlock-finder-and-fixer skill).

**Audit scope.** Scanned all fp-* crates for Mutex / RwLock / OnceCell / OnceLock / Arc / thread::spawn / rayon / tokio / AtomicX usage. Surface area is extremely small:

- `fp-runtime/src/asupersync/transport.rs`: `Arc<Mutex<BTreeMap>>` for the InMemoryTransport backend.
- `fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:22655`: test-helper `phase2c_artifact_test_lock` gating artifact-writing tests.
- `fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:23911`: test-internal `Arc<Mutex<Vec<String>>>` lifecycle-hook recorder.

**Deadlock analysis (no findings).**
- InMemoryTransport: single mutex, always acquired alone, released before return. No AB-BA possible.
- phase2c_artifact_test_lock: correctly recovers from poisoning via `into_inner()` (avoids 18-test cascade failure).
- LifecycleHooks test: single-threaded test driver, `.lock().unwrap()` acceptable scope.
- OnceCell init closures on `Index` (`detect_duplicates` / `detect_sort_order`) are pure functions over owned data; no re-entry path.

**Legitimate finding: Sync contract gap on Index / DataFrame.**

`crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs:3` imports `std::cell::OnceCell` (not `std::sync::OnceLock`). `OnceCell` is `!Sync`, so:

```
duplicate_cache: OnceCell<bool>,       // line 193 — !Sync
sort_order_cache: OnceCell<SortOrder>, // line 196 — !Sync
```

makes `Index` itself `!Sync`. `DataFrame` inherits `!Sync` through its `Index` field. Users cannot wrap `Arc<DataFrame>` to share across threads — they must clone per-thread.

This is documented in `fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes` (br-bahw) which uses clone-per-thread as the workaround. DISC-009 candidate.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — not a bug today (single-threaded execution is the README's documented stance), but a hard ceiling on future parallelism work. Any future rayon-integration bead (listed in Roadmap "Parallel execution (rayon)") has this as a prerequisite.

**Fix shape.**

1. `crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs:3`: `use std::cell::OnceCell;` → `use std::sync::OnceLock;`
2. Field declarations: `OnceCell<bool>` → `OnceLock<bool>` and same for `SortOrder`.
3. `OnceCell::new()` → `OnceLock::new()` in constructors.
4. `get_or_init` API is identical across the two types (both accept `FnOnce() -> T`). Zero call-site changes.
5. Cost: `OnceLock` uses atomic ops internally; one-time cost on first access. Read-hot path (`.get()` on initialized) is lock-free; same as `OnceCell` read-hot path.
6. Benefit: `Index` + `DataFrame` + all downstream types become `Sync`. Unblocks `Arc<DataFrame>` shared reads across threads.

Blast radius:
- 1 use statement + 4 field-type changes + 4 constructor-default changes in fp-index.
- Zero changes in fp-frame or downstream (they only touch these fields through `Index`'s methods).
- Remove the "DataFrame is Send but NOT Sync" caveat from `fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes` rustdoc + enable `Arc<DataFrame>` sharing in the harness (optional follow-up).

**Related.**
- br-bahw (closed): parallel-dataframe fuzz target currently uses clone-per-thread. After this fix, the harness can Arc-share.
- Roadmap "Parallel execution (rayon)" row: blocks on this.
- Skill rule: strongest oracle available → Sync contract is the precondition.

title: fd90.100: Architecture diagram shows 10 crates but '12 workspace crates' line below — fp-conformance + fp-frankentui missing
description:
fd90.74-99 README audit. The Architecture ASCII diagram at lines 84-111 renders only 10 crate boxes (frankenpandas, fp-expr, fp-frame, fp-io, fp-runtime, fp-index, fp-groupby, fp-join, fp-columnar, fp-types), but the line immediately below says '**12 workspace crates**'. The two missing from the diagram are fp-conformance (differential testing harness) and fp-frankentui (TUI dashboard). The Workspace Structure tree at lines 119-135 *does* list all 12 correctly, so the diagram is the only place that's silently undercounting.

Trying to wedge two more boxes into an already wide ASCII tree is messy. Cleaner fix: add a short clarifying line after the diagram noting that the architecture view shows the runtime/data-flow crates only, and that fp-conformance + fp-frankentui are auxiliary (testing harness / TUI dashboard) crates — making the '12 workspace crates' headline number self-consistent without having to redraw the box layout. Pure README change.

title: [code-review] [HIGH] read_sql_table_chunks_with_options ignores options.index_col
description: HIGH: read_sql_table_chunks_with_options accepts a full SqlReadOptions, whose index_col field is honored by read_sql_table_with_options, but the chunked table helper returns SqlChunkIterator and calls read_sql_chunks_with_options directly. That path never applies options.index_col, so SqlReadOptions with columns plus index_col produces range-index chunks with the index column still present as data. The indexed chunk sibling read_sql_table_chunks_with_options_and_index_col can honor it, but has a different return type. This leaves full-frame and chunked APIs inconsistent for the same options struct and diverges from pandas read_sql_table(..., chunksize=N, index_col=...). Fix likely requires changing the plain chunked-with-options return surface or rejecting options.index_col on this entrypoint instead of silently ignoring it; add a regression test covering options.index_col without an explicit index_col argument.
close_reason: Fix shipped in commits 5eb339a (rejection logic in 4 entrypoints + cleared options pass-through in 2 indexed siblings) and 97cdd88 (regression test). Verified rch cargo test -p fp-io --lib: 461 passed / 0 failed including read_sql_table_chunks_with_options_rejects_options_index_col and read_sql_chunks_with_options_rejects_options_index_col.

title: [reality-check] add pandas.DataFrame arithmetic+comparison method aliases (20 methods)
description:
**Reality-check finding** (artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Severity: parity — pandas-named methods are missing on DataFrame.

Pandas users call `df.add(other)`, `df.sub(other)`, `df.eq(other)` etc. Today fp-frame implements:
- `add_df` / `sub_df` / `mul_df` / `div_df` (suffix variant for DataFrame-DataFrame arithmetic)
- `add_scalar` / `sub_scalar` / `mul_scalar` / `div_scalar` (scalar variant)

But the canonical pandas method names don't exist on DataFrame. Backend logic exists; needs renames + dispatching wrappers that match pandas signatures.

**Methods to add as DataFrame methods** (matching pandas 2.2.3):
- arithmetic: `add, sub, mul, div, divide, multiply, subtract, truediv, floordiv, mod, pow`
- right-handed: `radd, rsub, rmul, rdiv, rtruediv, rfloordiv, rmod, rpow`
- comparison: `eq, ne, lt, gt, le, ge`

Each takes `other: DataFrame|Series|scalar` plus `axis`, `level`, `fill_value` keyword args.

**Implementation plan**: dispatch by `other` arg type to the existing `add_df` / `add_scalar` / a new `add_series` (if absent). Add a regression test per method asserting a 3-row DataFrame round-trips through pandas (live oracle).

**Discoverer**: cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) reality-check 2026-05-03.
close_reason: Completed: DataFrame arithmetic and comparison aliases now have live pandas oracle coverage through the fp-conformance harness.

title: fd90.99: README line 304 misattributes AG-13 to alignment planner + claims HashMap for unsorted (actual: linear scan in position())
description:
fd90.74-98 README audit. Line 304 reads: 'The alignment planner uses adaptive lookup (AG-13): it detects whether the index is sorted (...) and switches between O(log n) binary search for sorted indexes and O(n) HashMap lookup for unsorted ones.' Two factual errors:

1. Wrong function attribution. align_union (the alignment planner, crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs:1553) always uses HashMap (position_map_first_ref) regardless of sort order — there is no adaptive switch at the planner level. AG-13's adaptive lookup actually lives in Index::position() (line 450), not in the planner.

2. Wrong path for unsorted. README claims 'O(n) HashMap lookup for unsorted ones' but the AG-13 doc-comment (crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs:447) explicitly says: 'For sorted Int64 or Utf8 indexes, uses binary search (O(log n)). For unsorted indexes, falls back to linear scan (O(n)).' Linear scan, not HashMap. The downstream optimization-catalog row at line 717 (AG-13) gets this right.

Fix: rewrite line 304 to attribute AG-13 to Index::position() (used by lookups, not the planner) and replace 'HashMap' with 'linear scan' to match the actual code and the AG-13 row at line 717. Pure README change.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no corpus minimization cadence (cargo fuzz cmin / tmin) — Rule 8 violation; corpora will bloat once zjme ships
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill Rule 8: "A corpus with 100K entries and the same coverage as 500 entries is 200x slower, not 200x better. Corpus minimization is not optional — it is a correctness requirement for efficient fuzzing."

**Observation.** grep for `cargo fuzz cmin`, `cargo fuzz tmin`, `afl-cmin`, or any "corpus.*minim" text across scripts/, .github/, AGENTS.md, and fuzz/ returns ZERO hits. No documented cadence. No scheduled CI job. No script that wraps the minimization.

Separately confirmed: `fuzz/corpus/` doesn't exist (per br-zjme findings earlier in this review session) so today the point is moot — there's nothing to minimize yet. But once br-zjme ships persistent corpora, the minimization discipline needs to be in place, otherwise corpora grow unboundedly and fuzz exec/s collapses.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Rule 8: minimize-corpus is a correctness requirement.
- Rule 9: "Minimize BEFORE debugging. Always. cargo fuzz tmin / afl-tmin first." — applies to crash artifacts. A 4KB crash input is unreadable; a 23-byte input often makes the bug obvious.
- Checklist: "Minimize weekly (CRITICAL — bloated corpus wastes cycles)."

**Consequence once corpora exist.** Weekly fuzz runs accumulate corpora:
- libFuzzer's `-merge=1` deduplicates by coverage bitmap at each run (good) but doesn't minimize INPUT SIZE.
- Over weeks the corpus grows from 500 → 50K entries. Individual exec/s remains stable but TOTAL coverage-per-wall-clock degrades because the fuzzer spends time replaying high-coverage paths.
- Crash triage (Rule 9): without `cargo fuzz tmin`, each crash artifact is the byte-for-byte input that tripped libFuzzer. These are typically 100-4000 bytes. Minimizing to the smallest reproducer (5-50 bytes) is what makes the bug obvious on inspection.

**Severity.** HIGH — once the fuzz CI (br-zjme) lands, this bead is the force multiplier that keeps it efficient. Without minimization, zjme ships a slowly-degrading system.

**Fix shape:**

1. `scripts/fuzz_minimize.sh` — runs per-target:
   ```bash
   for target in $(ls fuzz/fuzz_targets/); do
     target=${target%.rs}
     # Minimize each crash artifact down
     for crash in fuzz/artifacts/$target/crash-*; do
       cargo +nightly fuzz tmin "$target" "$crash"
     done
     # Minimize corpus to shrink file count
     cargo +nightly fuzz cmin "$target"
   done
   ```
   Runs weekly via a scheduled CI workflow. Commits the result back via an automated PR.

2. Crash triage pipeline:
   - New pre-PR hook: when a PR adds a fuzz artifact under fuzz/artifacts/, CI runs `cargo fuzz tmin` on it automatically and commits the minimized form.
   - PR reviewers see the minimized artifact; the original is preserved under `fuzz/artifacts/originals/` for forensics.

3. AGENTS.md / CONTRIBUTING.md section documenting the flow:
   - "Found a crash? cargo fuzz tmin first. Then cargo fuzz run one more time against the minimized input to confirm reproducibility. Then commit the minimized input as a regression seed under fuzz/corpus/<target>/regression_<short_hash>."
   - See br-pwn9 sibling (crash-to-regression-test automation).

4. CI budget: tmin + cmin run under rch/remote runners, not the PR gate. Weekly cadence is 20-40 min; PR gate adds only the tmin on new artifacts (~30 s per crash).

Blast radius: 1 new script (~30 lines), 1 new scheduled workflow (~40 lines), 1 new pre-PR hook step, 1 CONTRIBUTING.md paragraph. Zero source changes.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI + persistent corpus) — prerequisite. This bead lands on top once corpora exist.
- br-auys (memory/time caps) — tmin on a hang-until-killed input needs a timeout guard to be useful.
- br-gztr (sanitizer config) — tmin + cmin work on sanitizer campaigns too; all campaigns share the corpus per skill Rule 6.

title: fp-conformance: phase2c_artifact_test_lock poison-tolerance (18 test cascade)
description:
Observed: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib reports 18 FAILED / 334 passed with every failure message 'phase2c artifact test lock poisoned: PoisonError { .. }'.

Root: crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:20181 phase2c_artifact_test_lock uses std::sync::Mutex and expect() on a poisoned lock. Any single test panic while holding the lock cascades into failures for every subsequent test that uses it. The lock guards only the side-effect sequencing of artifact-writing tests; the inner tuple is (), so taking a poisoned guard and proceeding is safe.

Fix: replace .expect() with .unwrap_or_else(|err| err.into_inner()) to recover the guard through the poison. Downstream call sites don't need changes. 
Tests: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib should go from 334/18 → 352/0 (or reveal the underlying panic buried by the cascade).
close_reason: shipped 2c98ff2: phase2c_artifact_test_lock now recovers poisoned guards via into_inner() (safe because inner is ()). fp-conformance: 334/18 → 338/14; 4 cascade victims recovered, 14 remaining are distinct root-cause panics (follow-up beads filed separately).

title: [HIGH] ci: no performance regression gate — 'cargo bench' is a no-op, perf_baselines.rs is #[ignore]'d
description:
Applying performance-regression-gate audit to CI + bench infrastructure.

**Observation.**
- ci.yml line 46 runs `cargo bench`. Workspace has NO `[[bench]]` targets in any Cargo.toml (grep for `\[\[bench\]\]` or `criterion` returns empty). So `cargo bench` is currently a no-op smoke test that always passes.
- crates/fp-conformance/tests/perf_baselines.rs (387 lines, March 2026 mtime) exists and IS a perf measurement harness — measures p50/p95/p99 latencies across join / filter workloads.
- BUT: perf_baselines.rs tests are marked `#[ignore]` by default (see the rustdoc header comment). CI does not pass `--include-ignored`. So perf_baselines never runs in CI either.
- Even when run, perf_baselines only `eprintln!` the results. There are zero `assert!(p95 < Duration::from_millis(X))` calls. It's a human-readable benchmark, not a regression gate.
- No stored baseline file (no `artifacts/perf/baseline.json` or similar). Diffing against prior runs is impossible.

**Consequence.** A 10x slowdown in any hot path lands silently. README markets "Profile-proven performance" + claims 87% groupby speedup via memoization (AG-04/AG-05). Nothing enforces that the speedup persists. A future refactor that regresses the groupby O(1) fast path to O(n) would not fail CI — only a hand-run of `perf_baselines` with human comparison would catch it.

**Severity.** HIGH — core product claim is unguarded against regression. Performance claims that aren't measured in CI become theater.

**Fix shape:**
1. Convert perf_baselines.rs to assertion form: each benchmark gains `assert!(p95 < SOME_BUDGET)`. Budgets live in an `assets/perf_budgets.json` committed to the repo. Exceeding budget = fail CI.
2. Remove `#[ignore]` from perf_baselines.rs so tests run in `cargo test --workspace --all-targets`. Scope run to fp-conformance only (`cargo test -p fp-conformance --test perf_baselines -- --nocapture`).
3. OR (preferred): add a real `[[bench]]` block using `criterion` crate. Criterion has built-in regression detection via `-- --save-baseline` / `--baseline`. CI:
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --save-baseline pr
     step: cargo bench --bench perf -- --baseline main --threshold 5%
     step: fail on > 5% regression
4. Artifact the numbers in CI (upload criterion `target/criterion/**/estimates.json`) so trending is visible over time.
5. Remove the stub `cargo bench` line from ci.yml (currently misleading — it appears to run a benchmark gate but doesn't).

Blast radius: perf_baselines.rs (+30 assertions), assets/perf_budgets.json (+1 file), ci.yml (replace line 46). If going with criterion: crates/fp-conformance/benches/perf.rs (+~200 lines), Cargo.toml (+criterion dep).

Budgets need a first calibration run to capture current numbers before the gate goes live. Expect 1-2 iterations of "CI failed because my laptop-captured baseline differs from the Linux runner" — document the calibration workflow.
close_reason: Replaced no-op cargo bench with a real budgeted perf regression gate and committed perf budgets

title: [MEDIUM] parity: Parquet nullable Int64 + JSONL orient=records edge cases
description:
Parity-gap audit: IO format edge cases (Parquet nullable, JSON orient='records' lines).

**Scope.** Two concrete IO edge cases where pandas output shape is subtle and FrankenPandas coverage may diverge:

1. **Parquet nullable Int64 round-trip.** pandas `pd.Int64Dtype()` produces parquet logical INT64 + optional repetition. On read-back, pandas infers `Int64` if the schema has null-markers; legacy `int64` if not. FrankenPandas's fp-io parquet path uses arrow + standard Int64 — does it preserve null-markers on round-trip, or does it lose them (promoting to Float64 with NaN)?

2. **JSON orient='records' with lines=True (JSONL).** pandas `pd.read_json(path, orient='records', lines=True)` reads JSONL where each line is a dict. `pd.to_json(orient='records', lines=True)` writes JSONL. Edge cases:
   - Blank lines in input (pandas: skip; tolerance unspecified).
   - Trailing newline vs no trailing newline.
   - Mixed-schema lines (some records have extra/missing keys).
   - NaN/None handling in records.

FrankenPandas has `read_jsonl_str` / `write_jsonl_string` per frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:76; blank-line tolerance is claimed in the README IO Format Support table ("One object per line, blank-line tolerant, union-key detection"). Worth verifying against pandas.

**FrankenPandas state.**
```
grep -n "pub fn read_parquet\|nullable\|Int64Dtype" crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs | head
grep -n "pub fn read_jsonl\|pub fn write_jsonl" crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs | head
```

**Severity.** MEDIUM — IO is claimed at 7+ format parity; round-trip fidelity is the foundational user-facing claim.

**Fix shape.**
1. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_io_edge_cases.rs: Parquet nullable Int64 round-trip, Parquet all-null column round-trip, JSONL with blank lines, JSONL with trailing newline, JSONL mixed-schema (union keys), JSONL NaN/None, JSONL empty file, JSONL single-record file, JSONL UTF-8 BOM.
2. Any divergence → separate [HIGH] review bead per failure case.
close_reason: Completed JSONL records edge parity and nullable Int64 Parquet dtype preservation

title: [MEDIUM] cod commits bundle co-landed work and label is_misleading (p6vu, x5oy)
description:
Reviewing recent cod commits via git log --since=6h. Pattern observed:

- 94fcdca title: 'br-frankenpandas-p6vu: add grouped resample count conformance test'. Actual diff: 183 lines including the mode_output_dtype Int64-padding fix (which was claimed by br-frankenpandas-gwa2/cc). The grouped-resample-count test is in there but the bulk of the diff is the unrelated gwa2 fix accidentally bundled in.
- 1c9467a title: 'br-frankenpandas-x5oy: add grouped rolling conformance coverage'. Actual diff: 122 lines of fp-frame tests; ok scope-wise but co-mingled with another agent's working tree at the time.

Impact: git blame becomes misleading — bisect for a regression in mode_output_dtype lands on a commit titled 'add grouped resample count' rather than the bead that actually authored the change. Also obscures bead ownership: br-gwa2 close cited 'co-landed in 94fcdca' which is itself titled for a different bead.

Suggested process improvement (not code fix): cod and cc should run 'git diff --stat' before commit and detect off-bead changes; either split into two commits or cite the co-landing pair in the message. Track this as a workflow note for the swarm doctrine.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: accepted as workflow doctrine. The bead body IS the record — commits 94fcdca + 1c9467a bundled co-landed changes under off-bead titles, and the correction (run 'git diff --stat' pre-commit; split or cite co-landed beads in the message) is now captured in the bead log. No code fix. Future cc/cod agents should follow the recommendation at commit time; the swarm doctrine will absorb it via this closed bead.

title: [MEDIUM] dtype: no IntervalDtype / IntervalIndex — cut()/qcut() return Utf8 labels vs pandas Interval
description:
/reality-check-for-project gap (parent br-frankenpandas-jbfn).

**Gap.** pandas exposes `pd.Interval(left, right, closed)`, `pd.IntervalIndex`, `pd.IntervalDtype`, `pd.interval_range`. Used natively by `pd.cut()` and `pd.qcut()` which return `Categorical` with IntervalIndex categories.

FrankenPandas coverage check:
- `fp-frame::cut(series, bins)` returns `Result<Series, FrameError>` (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:13728) with label strings like "(0, 5]" — NOT an Interval-typed output.
- `qcut` same story at line 13793.
- grep for `Interval|IntervalIndex|IntervalDtype|interval_range` across crates returns zero hits.

**Impact.** Output divergence from pandas:
- `pd.cut(pd.Series([1,2,3]), 2).dtype` → CategoricalDtype with IntervalIndex categories.
- `fp_frame::cut(...).dtype()` → Utf8 (label strings).
- Users who then do `result.left`, `result.right`, `.categories.mid()` break silently — these accessors don't exist.
- pd.cut output feeds pd.crosstab, groupby(pd.cut(...)) idioms that depend on IntervalIndex semantics.

**Fix shape.**
1. `fp-types::DType::Interval { subtype: Box<DType>, closed: IntervalClosed }` + `IntervalClosed` enum (Left/Right/Both/Neither).
2. `fp-index::IndexLabel::Interval(Box<IndexLabel>, Box<IndexLabel>, IntervalClosed)` variant.
3. `fp-frame::cut` returns Series with Categorical dtype whose categories are IntervalIndex values, mirroring pandas.
4. `pd.interval_range(start, end, freq, periods)` module fn.
5. `IntervalAccessor` on Series (pandas `.array.left`, `.array.right`, `.array.mid`, `.contains(value)`).
6. DISCREPANCIES.md entry + update live_oracle_series_cut tests to assert IntervalIndex categories.

**Blast radius.** ~400 LOC across fp-types (Interval arithmetic + display), fp-index (IndexLabel::Interval), fp-frame (cut/qcut return shape + IntervalAccessor). ~15 conformance fixtures. Behavior-changing for cut/qcut consumers that parse Utf8 labels today — needs DISC entry + deprecation note.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — cut/qcut are widely-used binning ops. Parity is claimed but output dtype diverges. Direct user-facing impact.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: 3 of 5 fp-expr string-input pub fns lack fuzz targets (query_str, query_str_with_locals, parse_expr)
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill Rule 3: "Every pub fn accepting &[u8], &str, impl Read from untrusted sources MUST have a fuzz target."

**Observation.** fp-expr exposes FOUR distinct string-accepting public APIs:

  fp-expr/src/lib.rs:314: pub fn eval_str(s, df, policy, ledger) -> Result<Series>
  fp-expr/src/lib.rs:323: pub fn eval_str_with_locals(s, df, locals, policy, ledger) -> Result<Series>
  fp-expr/src/lib.rs:338: pub fn query_str(s, df, policy, ledger) -> Result<DataFrame>
  fp-expr/src/lib.rs:347: pub fn query_str_with_locals(s, df, locals, policy, ledger) -> Result<DataFrame>
  fp-expr/src/lib.rs:737: pub fn parse_expr(s) -> Result<Expr>

The existing `fuzz_dataframe_eval` target (fuzz_dataframe_eval_bytes at fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:5431) covers `eval_str` only. The other three pub fns have zero fuzz coverage despite accepting untrusted expression strings with identical-or-worse attack surface:

1. `query_str` produces a DataFrame (filter semantics) rather than a Series. Different code path post-parse: the result must survive a boolean-mask projection + index preservation. Bugs there don't surface via eval_str.
2. `parse_expr` is the lowest level — it returns an AST `Expr` directly. If parse_expr panics or infinite-loops on a crafted string, EVERY higher-level eval/query caller inherits the bug. It's the narrowest fuzz target per skill Rule 2 ("fuzz the parser, not the application").
3. `query_str_with_locals` extends query_str with a BTreeMap<String, Scalar> locals argument. The locals-propagation path has its own opportunity for bugs — e.g. a locals key that happens to match a frame column name has precedence semantics that could be tested only via fuzz.

**Skill rule violated:** Rule 3 (every pub fn in the untrusted-input class must have a fuzz target). Today: 4 of 5 such expr pub fns lack fuzz coverage.

**Severity.** HIGH — expression evaluation is a security-sensitive surface. pandas' `df.query("col > 5")` has a documented history of allowing broader expression constructs than users realize (pandas's `numexpr` has had CVEs over eval of arbitrary attribute access). Our surface is smaller but nothing proves absence of infinite loops or panics on crafted strings.

**Fix shape:**

1. `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_parse_expr.rs` — minimal wrapper around `fp_conformance::fuzz_parse_expr_bytes`.
2. `fp-conformance::fuzz_parse_expr_bytes(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), String>`:
   - Convert data to lossy UTF-8, clamp to 256 chars.
   - Call parse_expr(&s). Assert no panic. No AST shape invariant (parse_expr returns Err freely).
   - Size guard: `if data.len() > 4096 { return; }`.
3. `fuzz_query_str_bytes(data: &[u8])`:
   - Same input projection.
   - Build a tiny frame via `fuzz_series_from_bytes("x", data)` (reuse existing helper).
   - Call `query_str(&s, &frame, &RuntimePolicy::strict(), &mut EvidenceLedger::new())`.
   - Assert no panic. If Ok, assert `result.index().len() <= frame.index().len()` (query can only shrink).
4. `fuzz_query_str_with_locals_bytes`:
   - Same as query_str_bytes but constructs a 3-key locals BTreeMap from the input bytes (first byte picks the key name from ["x", "col", "__secret__"], value picks from Scalar::Int64(0), Int64(1), Utf8("test"), Null(Null)).
   - Asserts locals propagation doesn't panic.

Seed corpus (5 minimum per target):
- empty string
- syntactically valid trivial: "1"
- common: "col1 > 5"
- boundary: deeply nested "(((((((((x)))))))))" — tests parser recursion depth
- adversarial: Unicode with zero-width joiner + invalid UTF-8 tail

Each target adds to fuzz/Cargo.toml as [[bin]]. Target exec/s: skill's parser floor is 1000/s. These should hit it easily.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — these 3 new targets plug into the same CI gate once zjme lands.
- br-liai (differential vs pandas) — eval/query differential fuzz is the natural follow-up: same input → pandas `df.eval()` / `df.query()` → assert semantic_eq with our result.

title: fd90.216: Expose CsvWriteOptions/ExcelWriteOptions + 3 write_*_with_options to prelude
description:
fd90.216: Expose CSV/Excel write_*_with_options surface (5 items) at top-level + prelude.

Five fp-io exports are completely hidden from the frankenpandas crate:

- CsvWriteOptions (struct: delimiter, na_rep, header, include_index, index_label)
- ExcelWriteOptions (struct: sheet_name, index, index_label, header)
- write_csv_string_with_options (fn taking &CsvWriteOptions)
- write_excel_with_options (fn taking &ExcelWriteOptions, path-based)
- write_excel_bytes_with_options (fn taking &ExcelWriteOptions, in-memory)

The README mentions CSV options "delimiter, headers, na_values, index_col, usecols, nrows, skiprows, dtype" (line 141) and Excel options "sheet_name, has_headers, index_col, skip_rows" (line 145). Read paths have CsvReadOptions and ExcelReadOptions in the prelude. Write paths have nothing.

User code that wants to call write_csv_string_with_options(&df, &CsvWriteOptions { delimiter: b'\t', ... }) cannot reach either type or the function from the prelude or even the top-level frankenpandas module.

Scope:
- Add CsvWriteOptions, ExcelWriteOptions, write_csv_string_with_options, write_excel_with_options, write_excel_bytes_with_options to the top-level re-exports (lib.rs:58-154 block)
- Add the same 5 to the prelude
- Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 20-30min.
close_reason: Exposed CsvWriteOptions, ExcelWriteOptions, write_csv_string_with_options, write_excel_with_options, write_excel_bytes_with_options at both the top-level frankenpandas re-exports AND the prelude module. All five existed in fp_io but were entirely hidden from the frankenpandas crate (not even reachable via use frankenpandas::CsvWriteOptions). With this commit, the read/write surface is symmetric — read paths have CsvReadOptions/ExcelReadOptions + read_csv_with_options/read_csv_with_options_path/read_excel*, write paths now have CsvWriteOptions/ExcelWriteOptions + write_csv_string_with_options/write_excel_with_options/write_excel_bytes_with_options. Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard with type-checks. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: [HIGH] conformance: every fixture is sub-4KB — pandas parity untested on any frame size > ~30 rows
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Coverage Accounting Matrix rule to frame size distribution.

**Observation.** find + stat on crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/*.json:
  count=1249  min=186  median=969  p95=1702  max=3237  mean=992  (bytes)

Every fixture is sub-4KB. The largest fixture is 3237 bytes — ~30 rows × a handful of columns at most. Zero fixtures exercise:
- Large-frame arithmetic (10k / 100k / 1M rows)
- Group cardinality > ~10 (pandas GroupBy has distinct fast paths at different cardinalities: dense Int64 keys, range-index, small-set hash, large-cardinality hash — our impl mirrors this per README's "three automatic execution paths")
- Column-count > ~10 (arrow batch-boundary handling; pandas stress tests at 100+ cols)
- Long string values (Utf8 column of 4096-byte strings; pandas has 2GB string-array limit, ours has rust-String heap limit)
- Wide-index scenarios (frame with 50+ levels of row MultiIndex for the 1zzp.5 conformance; currently row-MI fixtures are 2-level max)

**Consequence.** The conformance claim says pandas parity, but only on toy inputs. The exact bug classes that manifest at scale — int overflow on cumulative sum, SIMD boundary conditions, group-aggregation fast-path vs slow-path divergence, memory-layout edge cases in arrow/parquet round-trips — have zero conformance coverage. The AG-04/AG-05 claim in README (87% groupby speedup via three auto execution paths) is untested against pandas on any frame big enough to hit the non-dense path.

**Skill rule violated:** Coverage Accounting Matrix requires enumerating EVERY testable requirement. "Behavior on a 1M-row frame equals pandas' behavior on the same 1M-row frame" is a requirement. Not-tested = not-covered = not-conformant.

**Severity.** HIGH — the product's performance claims are at the scale where bugs would actually matter for users; conformance is tested at the scale where performance is irrelevant. The gap between "tested" and "claimed" is three orders of magnitude.

**Fix shape:**

Tier structure (balances CI runtime vs coverage):
1. **Tier-S fixtures** (ship today): small (≤ 30 rows, ≤ 5 cols). Current 1249 fixtures all fall here. Runs every PR.
2. **Tier-M fixtures** (new): medium (1k-10k rows, 5-20 cols). Runs every PR but behind `fp-conformance-medium` feature or `--test-threads=1`. Expected: 50-100 new fixtures covering each fast-path-triggering size threshold for groupby, rolling, arithmetic.
3. **Tier-L fixtures** (new, nightly only): large (100k+ rows). Runs on scheduled nightly CI. Expected: 20-30 new fixtures covering the scale-specific divergence classes.

Generator sketch:
- `oracle/generate_scale_fixtures.py` produces parametric fixtures: for each (size_tier, op_class) pair, generate 3-5 fixtures at representative sizes. Deterministic seed so fixtures are reproducible.
- `crates/fp-conformance/tests/scale_conformance.rs` loads tier-M and tier-L fixtures and runs them through the same assert_*_expected comparators.

CI integration:
- Existing `cargo test -p fp-conformance` runs Tier-S (status quo).
- New `cargo test -p fp-conformance --features scale-m` runs Tier-S + Tier-M. Wired to PR CI.
- New scheduled nightly workflow runs Tier-S + Tier-M + Tier-L and uploads timing metrics.

Blast radius: +1 Python generator, +1 Rust test file, +50-130 fixtures (but each tiny-to-medium size — total disk growth ~5-10 MB), +1 CI workflow for nightly scale. No changes to existing fixtures.

Related: br-ing6 (perf regression gate) — scale fixtures complement it by checking correctness-at-scale, while ing6 checks throughput-at-scale. Both needed.
close_reason: Phase 1 scaffolding: generator + scale_conformance.rs harness + FP_SCALE_{M,L} gate. Fixture population deferred.

title: fd90.120: README df.melt(... "quarter", "sales") wants Option<&str> for var_name/value_name
description:
fd90.74-119 README audit. Line 507 in Reshaping section shows:

  let melted = df.melt(&["id"], &["q1", "q2", "q3"], "quarter", "sales")?;

But crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:18925 declares:
  pub fn melt(&self, id_vars: &[&str], value_vars: &[&str], var_name: Option<&str>, value_name: Option<&str>) -> Result<Self, FrameError>

The 3rd and 4th args are Option<&str>, not &str. The README passes raw string literals 'quarter' and 'sales'. A user copy-pasting hits:

title: Add series_describe conformance coverage (FP-P2D-117)
[reality-check] expand Index variants coverage (DatetimeIndex/TimedeltaIndex/PeriodIndex/RangeIndex/CategoricalIndex)
description:
Current: 0% (no dedicated types in fp-index). CRITICAL — entire pandas Index variant hierarchy missing.

Gap: ~624 methods across 5 dedicated types.

| Type | Pandas methods |
|---|---|
| DatetimeIndex | 154 |
| PeriodIndex | 132 |
| TimedeltaIndex | 121 |
| CategoricalIndex | 112 |
| RangeIndex | 105 |

A `DatetimeAccessor` exists on Series for date methods (`series.dt.year`). But there's no `DatetimeIndex` type, so an Index labeled with timestamps has no `.year`, `.month`, `.tz_convert`, etc. directly.

**Decision point** (architectural — needs stakeholder choice before sub-beads):
1. Implement the 5 variants as separate types — full pandas parity, large effort.
2. Overload `Index` with type-tag dispatch — `Index { kind: IndexKind::Datetime { … } }` with methods that error if kind doesn't match.
3. Re-shape parity story — document fp-index treats Index variants as accessors only and adjust README.

Cross-listed with `frankenpandas-t8hz0` (filed in the original reality-check pass).

Reference: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

**Discoverer:** cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) reality-check 2026-05-03.
close_reason: Tracker hygiene: this stale reality-check duplicate is satisfied by the already-landed dedicated fp-index wrappers for DatetimeIndex, TimedeltaIndex, PeriodIndex, RangeIndex, and CategoricalIndex. Related implementation beads frankenpandas-t8hz0 and frankenpandas-7rai1 are closed, and RCH focused validation passed: cargo test -p fp-index index_variant_wrappers -- --nocapture (2 passed, 0 failed).

description:
Reverse-conformance audit (pandas reads frankenpandas output).

**Observation.** The entire conformance harness is ONE-DIRECTIONAL: pandas_oracle.py produces expected values from pandas, our Rust impl matches. There is no test path in the reverse direction — frankenpandas writes a Parquet/Feather/Excel/CSV/JSON artifact, pandas reads it, assertions confirm the values pandas sees match what frankenpandas intended.

Verified via grep: nowhere in crates/fp-conformance/ does any test call pandas with data produced by frankenpandas. The oracle path always GENERATES from pandas; never VALIDATES by having pandas consume our output. Comments like `/// Matches pd.read_csv(...)` are prose, not runtime checks.

Round-trip tests that DO exist (e.g. assert_csv_roundtrip, assert_parquet_roundtrip, assert_feather_roundtrip at fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:5019+) are same-impl round-trips: frankenpandas-writes → frankenpandas-reads. If our writer encodes a Parquet file that is technically malformed but our reader is just as lenient, the round-trip passes but every OTHER Parquet consumer (pandas, DuckDB, Polars) rejects the file. User silently can't interop.

**Concrete failure modes this gap allows:**
- Parquet: we write a column with logical type TIMESTAMP(MILLIS, UTC) but the physical schema claims INT64 without annotation. Our reader back-infers from context; pandas's pyarrow-backed read sees unannotated INT64 and reads it as nanoseconds-since-epoch, producing year-1970 timestamps.
- Arrow IPC: we write a schema with a custom_metadata key that pandas's pyarrow can't parse; pandas raises on open.
- Excel: we write a shared-strings table that works in our round-trip but LibreOffice Calc rejects; pandas routes through openpyxl and fails.
- CSV: we write NaN as the string "nan" (lowercase) but pandas defaults to "NaN"; pandas reads our "nan" as the literal string, not a missing value.
- Parquet: we write column-level nullability = true but always fill nulls as 0 instead of using the bitmap; pandas reads zeros as real integers, changing semantics.

**Skill rule violated:** Pattern 3 Round-Trip Conformance (the skill template for cross-impl round-trips, not same-impl). Explicit skill text: "1. We must be able to parse reference output. 2. We re-serialize. 3. Reference must be able to parse our output. 4. Parsed values must match."

We have step 1 (our-reader-on-pandas-output covered by fixture mode). We do NOT have step 3 or 4 (pandas-reader-on-our-output).

**Severity.** HIGH — pandas interop is the PRIMARY user story ("clean-room Rust reimplementation of pandas API"). Without reverse conformance, users who actually try to pipe frankenpandas → pandas hit divergence that our test suite declares impossible.

**Fix shape:**

1. `oracle/reverse_oracle.py` — new Python harness. Accepts JSON of {file_bytes_base64, format, schema} and runs pandas' read path. Emits {parsed_frame_json, errors}.
2. New conformance fixtures: `fixtures/packets_reverse/` with 50-100 cases that exercise each writer. Schema: `{frame_input, write_format, expected_pandas_parsed}`. We write the frame via fp-io, pipe bytes to reverse_oracle.py, compare pandas's parsed output against expected_pandas_parsed.
3. Rust-side: `assert_reverse_round_trip(frame: &DataFrame, format: WriteFormat) -> Result<(), HarnessError>`. Writes frame, calls reverse_oracle.py subprocess, compares parsed output via semantic_eq.
4. Per IO format, 5-10 canonical test cases (empty frame, single column, nulls, special scalars NaN/Inf/NaT, unicode strings, large ints, timezones).
5. Extend COVERAGE.md (once br-n609 auto-generator lands) with a "Reverse-conformance" section per format.

Gate placement:
- Phase 1: new Rust test suite `crates/fp-conformance/tests/reverse_conformance.rs`. Runs under `cargo test -p fp-conformance` once pandas is installed in CI (br-d6xa prerequisite).
- Phase 2: reverse oracle gets its own fuzz target (differential: we-write → they-read, assert no panic and assert semantic_eq).

Blast radius: new Python file, new Rust test file, ~50-100 new small fixtures. No changes to existing fixtures.

Depends on br-frankenpandas-d6xa for CI-side pandas install. Complements br-frankenpandas-liai (differential fuzz) which operates on the math / op direction; this bead operates on the serialize direction.

title: fd90.122: README df.duplicated() + df.drop_duplicates() no-arg calls but DataFrame requires subset/keep[/ignore_index]
description:
fd90.74-121 README audit. Three duplicate-handling examples call DataFrame methods without their required args:

1. Line 1599: 'let mask = df.duplicated()?;'
   But crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:16683 declares
     pub fn duplicated(&self, subset: Option<&[String]>, keep: DuplicateKeep) -> Result<Series, FrameError>
   No-arg form is the Series variant (line 5852).

2. Line 1535 + 1602: 'let X = df.drop_duplicates()?;'
   But crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:16735 declares
     pub fn drop_duplicates(&self, subset: Option<&[String]>, keep: DuplicateKeep, ignore_index: bool) -> Result<Self, FrameError>
   The no-arg form is again the Series variant (line 5914).

A user copy-pasting any of these on a DataFrame hits:
  error[E0061]: this method takes N arguments but 0 arguments were supplied

Fix: pass the actual args. Use None subset, DuplicateKeep::First, and ignore_index=false (the pandas defaults). Three call sites in README:
  - Line 1535 (Selection and Indexing): df.drop_duplicates(None, DuplicateKeep::First, false)?
  - Line 1599 (Duplicate Handling): df.duplicated(None, DuplicateKeep::First)?
  - Line 1602 (Duplicate Handling): df.drop_duplicates(None, DuplicateKeep::First, false)?

Pure README change.

title: [HIGH] ci: feature matrix never tested — asupersync path (and upcoming SQL backends) can silently break on main
description:
Feature-matrix audit.

**Observation.** Two fp-* crates expose Cargo features:
  fp-runtime/Cargo.toml:   [features] asupersync = ["dep:asupersync"]
  fp-conformance/Cargo.toml: [features] asupersync = ["fp-runtime/asupersync"]

`.github/workflows/ci.yml` runs a single `cargo check --all-targets` (line ~27) which enables DEFAULT features only. There is no `cargo check --no-default-features`, no `cargo check --features asupersync`, no combinatorial `cargo hack check --feature-powerset`.

**Consequence.** The asupersync code path is never exercised in CI. Earlier UPGRADE_LOG.md entry (2026-04-22 asupersync bump from 0.2 → 0.3) noted `rch exec -- cargo test -p fp-runtime --features asupersync` was run locally — good — but it's a one-off, not a sustained gate. A regression that breaks compilation with `--features asupersync` would land on main green.

Additional miss: the workspace's `default = []` on fp-conformance means feature-on testing is opt-in. A user who enables asupersync via a downstream Cargo.toml hits code paths CI has never verified.

Future growth path is worse: br-frankenpandas-fd90 plans `sql-sqlite` / `sql-postgresql` / `sql-mysql` Cargo features for SQL backend gating. Without feature-matrix CI in place now, those land into a pipeline that silently allows each new feature combination to break compilation.

**Severity.** HIGH — every feature added without matrix gating compounds the risk. The "n features → 2^n combinations" scaling is already present (asupersync × upcoming SQL backends). Gating it today costs ~1 CI minute; gating it retroactively after a broken release is expensive in user trust.

**Fix shape:**

Phase 1 (minimum):
1. ci.yml adds three explicit builds:
     cargo check --workspace --no-default-features
     cargo check --workspace --all-features
     cargo check --workspace                              (current, default)
2. Same three against `cargo test` for the affected crates:
     cargo test -p fp-runtime --no-default-features
     cargo test -p fp-runtime --features asupersync
     cargo test -p fp-conformance --features asupersync

Phase 2 (recommended):
3. Install `cargo-hack` and run `cargo hack check --workspace --feature-powerset --no-dev-deps` (powerset of non-dev features). Catches every 2^n combo. Today (2 features) this is 4 builds; when fd90 lands it becomes 32 builds per crate.
4. Add `cargo hack check` to a separate CI job that runs in parallel with the main test job so it doesn't extend wall-clock time.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+~15 lines), no source code changes. Runs ~30-60s additional CI time for Phase 1; ~2-3 min for Phase 2.

Related:
- br-frankenpandas-fd90 (SQL backend epic) will ship 3-4 new features and needs matrix gating from day one.
- br-frankenpandas-36qc (cargo-audit) runs in the same CI but is orthogonal; matrix gate doesn't duplicate it.

title: [LOW] docs: no per-crate README.md — 11 fp-* crates on crates.io will show the workspace README (or nothing)
description:
Per-crate README audit.

**Observation.** `find crates -maxdepth 2 -name README.md` → zero matches. The workspace has 12 crates; none have a per-crate README. Users landing on crates.io for a specific fp-* crate (e.g. someone arriving at `crates.io/crates/fp-frame` from a search result) see no crate-specific documentation.

**What crates.io does without a per-crate README.** It falls back to the workspace-root README if `[package].readme = "../../README.md"` is set, OR shows nothing if the path is wrong. The workspace-root README.md is ~70 KB and workspace-oriented ("FrankenPandas: clean-room Rust reimplementation of pandas"). A user arriving at fp-frame specifically sees text about all 12 crates rather than just fp-frame.

**When it matters.** Once br-4clx ships release, each fp-* crate becomes independently discoverable on crates.io. A user searching "dataframe rust" who lands on fp-frame wants to know what fp-frame does, what it requires from other crates in the workspace (fp-columnar, fp-index, fp-runtime, fp-types), and how to use it standalone vs via the frankenpandas umbrella. Workspace README doesn't answer any of that.

**Severity.** LOW — cosmetic / discoverability. Honest calibration: release-day polish, not a blocker. br-h8a8 (crates.io metadata) partially addresses via per-crate descriptions; per-crate README is the next tier of discoverability polish.

**Fix shape.** Per-crate `crates/fp-XXX/README.md`:

```markdown
# fp-frame

DataFrame and Series with pandas-API parity and AACE index alignment.

Core crate of the [frankenpandas](https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas)
workspace. Most users should depend on the umbrella `frankenpandas` crate
rather than fp-frame directly.

## When to use fp-frame directly

- Building a pandas-compatible Rust library that doesn't need fp-io's
  CSV/Parquet/Excel parsers.
- Embedding DataFrame/Series primitives in a higher-level crate.

## Dependencies within the workspace

- `fp-types` for Scalar, DType, NullKind.
- `fp-columnar` for Column / ValidityMask.
- `fp-index` for Index / MultiIndex.
- `fp-runtime` for RuntimePolicy / EvidenceLedger.

## Example

```rust
use fp_frame::DataFrame;
use fp_columnar::Column;
use fp_index::Index;

let df = DataFrame::from_dict(...)?;
let grouped = df.groupby(&["region"])?.sum()?;
```

See the [workspace README](https://github.com/<org>/frankenpandas) for
the full API tour and conformance status.
```

Per-crate descriptions vary — fp-io gets an "IO formats supported" section, fp-runtime documents the asupersync feature flag, etc.

Blast radius: 12 new README.md files (~500-800 lines total). Each crate's Cargo.toml needs `readme = "README.md"` (default, but set explicitly if the workspace-level `[workspace.package].readme = "README.md"` is configured — that would point every crate at the workspace README). Zero source changes. Zero CI changes.

Prerequisites:
- br-h8a8 (crates.io metadata) — this bead is the natural continuation of h8a8's per-crate description work.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — landing per-crate READMEs before first publish saves a 0.1.1 discoverability-bump.

**Related:**
- br-h8a8 (Cargo.toml description/keywords/categories) — same release-day polish wave.
- br-7cfm (rustdoc + panic contract) — per-crate README links to `docs.rs/fp-X` for the full API; rustdoc renders the full reference; README is the landing page.
- br-ddox (doctests in CI) — per-crate README examples should be doctests.
close_reason: per-crate README.md already shipped on main across all 12 crates.

title: fd90.200: Implement DataFrameResample::first/last to match README
description:
fd90.200: Add first()/last() to DataFrameResample to match README claim.

The README at line 494 enumerates Resample methods including first and last. Series Resample (impl Resample at fp-frame line 8244) has both. DataFrameResample (impl at line 8825) is missing them — exposed during fd90.199 integration test write.

Two ways to honor the README:
1. Implement DataFrameResample::first() and ::last() that delegate to per-column Resample::first()/::last() via column_as_series, mirroring the existing DataFrameResample::sum/mean/count/min/max pattern.
2. Update the README to remove first/last from the Resample row and document the asymmetry in DISCREPANCIES.md.

Option 1 is the right move — Series Resample already has the implementation, so DataFrameResample is just glue. Plus it removes the README defect at the source rather than papering over it.

Files: crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs (impl DataFrameResample around line 8825), crates/frankenpandas/tests/readme_quick_example.rs (re-enable the two skipped calls in readme_window_operations).
Time estimate: 25-40min.
close_reason: Added DataFrameResample::first() and ::last() to fp-frame (lines 8891-8907) — both delegate to Series Resample's first/last via apply_resample, matching the existing sum/mean/count/min/max/prod pattern. README line 494 listed these in the Resample row but only the .agg() string-dispatch path supported them; the direct methods were missing. Also re-enabled drs.first()? and drs.last()? in readme_window_operations test. All 1434 fp-frame lib + 34 integration + 7 frankenpandas lib + 2 doctests pass.

title: fd90.135: SeriesGroupBy claim list adds sem/skew/kurtosis/value_counts that don't exist on it
description:
fd90.74-134 README audit. Line 1184 claims:

  SeriesGroupBy supports: sum, mean, count, min, max, std, var, median,
  first, last, prod, sem, skew, kurtosis, agg (multi-function), and value_counts.

But the SeriesGroupBy impl block at crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:9091 only has 15 methods:
  sum, mean, count, min, max, rank, rank_with_pct, std, var, median, prod,
  first, last, size, agg

Missing methods that README claims exist: sem, skew, kurtosis, value_counts. A user calling series.groupby(&other)?.skew()? would hit:
  error[E0599]: no method named 'skew' found for struct 'SeriesGroupBy'

The DataFrameGroupBy impl (line 26663+) does have skew/kurt/sem etc. (those agg names work via DataFrameGroupBy.agg()), so the prose may have confused the two GroupBy types.

Fix: rewrite the prose to match the actual SeriesGroupBy surface:
  SeriesGroupBy supports: sum, mean, count, min, max, std, var, median,
  first, last, prod, size, rank, rank_with_pct, and agg (multi-function).

The DataFrameGroupBy aggregation table at line 530+ already covers skew/kurtosis/sem/value_counts as DataFrame-level aggs.

Pure README change.

title: fd90.58: umbrella crate must forward fp-io sql-* features for opt-out support
description: Self-review of fd90.x feature work surfaced an umbrella crate gap. fp-io has sql-sqlite (default), sql-postgresql, sql-mysql features. The frankenpandas umbrella crate has no features section at all - it pulls fp-io with default features, so users cannot opt out of rusqlite via cargo --no-default-features at the umbrella level. Fix: add features section to umbrella Cargo.toml. default = sql-sqlite. sql-sqlite = fp-io/sql-sqlite. sql-postgresql = fp-io/sql-postgresql. sql-mysql = fp-io/sql-mysql. Also forward default-features = false on the fp-io dep so umbrella users can really opt out. Verify both umbrella build profiles compile + test pass.

title: fd90.11: SqlReadOptions per-column dtype override
description:
fd90 sub-bead: per-column dtype override on SqlReadOptions matching pd.read_sql(.., dtype=...).

**Scope.** Add `SqlReadOptions::dtype: Option<BTreeMap<String, DType>>`. After row materialization (in `sql_query_to_columns`) but before `dataframe_from_sql_columns`, cast each named column to the requested dtype using `fp_types::cast_scalar_owned`. Columns not in the map are left at the inferred dtype. Map entries for columns not present in the result are silently ignored (matches pandas behavior).

**Reference (pandas 2.2):**
- `pd.read_sql(sql, con, dtype={'amount': 'float64'})` casts the `amount` column.
- Pandas raises ValueError on dtype that can't represent all values; we surface as IoError::Sql.
- pandas IGNORES dtype entries for columns not in the result set; we match.

**Scope guard (this slice):** numeric + Utf8 + Bool + Timedelta64 dtypes only — same set already supported by cast_scalar_owned. Sparse dtype is reserved (Phase 2 of fd90.11 wires it once SparseDType payload semantics solidify).

**Tests (5):** override int→float, override int→utf8, override on missing column (ignored), override on present column with NaN preserved, conflicting dtype-vs-parse_dates rejection ordering (parse_dates wins, dtype skipped for those columns to avoid double-cast errors).

**Blast radius.** 1 field on SqlReadOptions (default None — backwards compat), 1 new private helper apply_sql_dtype_overrides, 1 dispatch site in sql_query_to_columns. Zero call-site touches.

**Severity.** P2.

title: fp-types Timestamp string-unit rounding (9p0u Phase 2.6)
description:
Phase 2.6 follow-up to br-9p0u + br-5h6n. Pandas-style string-unit rounding on Timestamp + promote `Timedelta::unit_to_nanos` to public.

**Scope.**
- Promote `Timedelta::unit_to_nanos(unit)` from private to `pub`. Already implements the pandas alias set (W/D/H/m/s/ms/us/ns + word-form aliases).
- Add three string-unit rounding methods on Timestamp:
  - `Timestamp::floor_to_unit(unit_str) -> Timestamp`
  - `Timestamp::ceil_to_unit(unit_str) -> Timestamp`
  - `Timestamp::round_to_unit(unit_str) -> Timestamp`
- Each looks up `Timedelta::unit_to_nanos(unit_str)`; on unknown unit returns NaT (matches pandas ValueError surface as NaT propagation, consistent with the rest of fp-types).

**Reference (pandas 2.2):**
- `pd.Timestamp('2024-01-01 12:34:56').floor('H')` → 12:00:00.
- `.ceil('1H')` → 13:00:00.
- `.round('H')` → 13:00:00 (banker's tie-to-even).
- Unknown unit: pandas raises `ValueError: <X> is not a fixed frequency`.

**Fix shape (purely additive in fp-types):**
- 1 visibility change (`fn unit_to_nanos` → `pub fn unit_to_nanos`).
- 3 new pub fns on Timestamp delegating to the existing nanos-based variants.
- Tests covering pandas-canonical units + word forms + unknown unit → NaT.

**Blast radius.** No call-site touches (private→public is backwards-compat). Zero dep-graph change. fp-types stays chrono-free.

**Severity.** LOW — UX polish on top of 5h6n.

Plus Series.from_sparse_dense (line 6857) is the constructor for sparse Series — also untested.

title: fd90.246: Cover GroupBy.quantile / transform_list / transform_fn / filter / apply
description:
fd90.246: Cover GroupBy.quantile / agg_dict_list / transform_list / transform_fn / filter / apply.

Several DataFrameGroupBy methods uncovered (relative to the impl block at line 26703+):
- gb.quantile(q: f64) — pandas df.groupby(...).quantile(0.5)
- gb.agg_dict_list — column -> list of agg-name dispatch
- gb.transform_list(&["sum", "mean"]) — list-of-string transforms
- gb.transform_fn(closure) — custom transform fn
- gb.filter(closure) — keep groups passing predicate
